If you've got a pre-2020 diesel with a timing chain rattle, you're facing the E-Pace's most common dilemma — repair the tensioner now, or wait for the failure and replace the whole engine. The economics box above shows replacement is viable, but the calculator will give you a personalised verdict based on your car's age, mileage, and value.

That's the difference between a knowledge base and a sales page.

SECTION 13 — E-PACE FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: E-Pace Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does an E-Pace engine replacement cost in the UK?
For the 2.0-litre Ingenium engines, reconditioned supply costs range from £3,000–£5,000 for the petrol (AJ200P) and £3,500–£5,800 for the diesel (AJ200D) [JAG-QUOTE]. Labour adds 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The P300e PHEV costs are not yet fully established in our data — we recommend a specialist quote for those. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised estimate → /fault-diagnostic-calculator.
Q2: Which E-Pace engine is the most reliable?
The AJ200P / 204PT Ingenium petrol has the cleanest record in our data — consistently outperforms the diesel in failure frequency, and the only meaningful weakness is water pump replacement (a routine job, not catastrophic). We recommend it for most buyers. [JAG-VERIFIED] — 500 enquiries across the JLR range confirm its popularity and reliability.
Q3: Which E-Pace engine should I avoid?
No engine is outright "avoid," but the pre-2020 AJ200D / 204DTD diesel carries a caveat — early tensioner wear can lead to timing chain failure if not addressed. Post-2020 cars with the revised design are much safer. If you're looking at a pre-2020 diesel, insist on documented tensioner inspection or a specialist check before buying. We'd also advise caution on the P300e without a full hybrid-system health check — the engine itself is solid, but the hybrid complexity adds risk.
Q4: Is the E-Pace timing chain problem real?
Yes — it's the same Ingenium diesel issue shared with Land Rover. Pre-2020 cars can experience tensioner wear, leading to chain stretch and eventual failure. Symptoms include a rattling noise on cold start, engine warning lights, and timing-related faults. If you hear that rattle, book an inspection immediately — catching it early means a tensioner replacement; ignoring it can mean a full engine. Post-2020 cars have a revised tensioner design and are far less prone.
Q5: Is it worth replacing an E-Pace engine?
Almost always yes. With the E-Pace valued at £12,000–£30,000 [THIRD-PARTY], even the most expensive reconditioned diesel (£5,800) sits at just 19–48% of the car's value — well below our 60% scrap threshold for Jaguar. That makes replacement viable across nearly every example, regardless of the engine. The only exception might be a very high-mileage, pre-2020 diesel with other issues — but even then, we'd suggest getting a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote.
Q6: Which E-Pace should I buy?
Buy a 2020+ E-Pace with the AJ200P / 204PT petrol. The post-facelift petrol Ingenium has the cleanest record in our data — no meaningful weakness beyond routine water pump replacement. The 2020 facelift also brought improved interior tech and revised suspension. If you genuinely need diesel economy, go for a 2020+ diesel (revised tensioner) and get a full service history check.
Q7: How do I find my E-Pace engine code?
The engine code is stamped on a plate on the engine block — often visible near the front or top. It's also listed on the V5C (logbook) and the VIN plate. For E-Pace models, the code will be one of: AJ200D, AJ200P, AJ20D6, AJ20P3 (P300e). If you're unsure, our diagnostic calculator can help identify it from your registration or symptoms → /fault-diagnostic-calculator.
Q8: E-Pace vs Evoque — which is more reliable?
Both share the same Ingenium engines and many mechanicals, but the E-Pace has a slightly cleaner reliability record in our data — the Evoque's earlier models (pre-2019) had more reported cooling and electrical issues. The E-Pace's later launch (2017) means most examples have the revised engineering from the start. However, both are safe buys post-2020. See our full comparison for the detailed verdict → E-Pace vs Evoque.

I-Pace
JAGUAR I-PACE — SECTIONS 1-7
Step 0 Classification (Internal Working Note)
Classification: First-Time Buyer / Gateway
Justification: The I-Pace is Jaguar's first and only fully electric vehicle — it attracts buyers new to both the Jaguar brand and EV ownership. The key "line that matters" is the 2021 model year update (Pivi Pro infotainment, improved range/efficiency), which separates early-adopter cars from the more refined later examples. This is a high-volume EV with a single generation but meaningful hardware/software revisions that owners research extensively.

★★★★½
Our Verdict: The I‑Pace is Jaguar's EV landmark. 2021 cars get Pivi Pro, improved battery management, and a rear motor bearing revision — these are the cars to target. Early cars are still good, but the infotainment alone makes the facelift worthwhile.
Explore I‑Pace X590 →

	The "Battery Health" Question — Same Car, Different Range
The I‑Pace's 90kWh battery degrades with age and use. A 2018 car might show 85–90% state of health; a 2021 car typically holds 92–96%. The difference can mean 15–25 miles of real-world range. Always ask for a battery health certificate before buying. Read the full comparison: Pre‑facelift vs Facelift I‑Pace →

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common Jaguar I‑Pace Problems
Sub-headline: What actually fails on the I‑Pace — battery, drive units, and the gremlins that come with being Jaguar's first EV.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	12V Battery Drain
	The I‑Pace's most common enquiry — modules failing to sleep correctly, killing the 12V auxiliary battery. Affects all years but can be mitigated with a battery tender.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	2
	Rear Motor Bearing Wear (Pre-2021)
	A documented weak point on earlier cars — bearing wear causes whining noise from the rear drive unit. Revised in 2021 with improved parts.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	Battery Degradation
	All EV batteries degrade — the I‑Pace's LG Chem 90kWh pack typically holds 85–95% SOH at 5–7 years. Range loss of 10–20 miles is normal; rapid degradation is a warranty claim.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	4
	Infotainment Lag (Pre-2021)
	Touch Pro Duo system is slow to boot, lags during operation, and sometimes crashes. Resolved with the 2021 Pivi Pro upgrade.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	5
	Charging Module / On-Board Charger Faults
	Some owners report AC charging faults — usually the on-board charger module failing to negotiate with home wallboxes. Software updates resolve most cases.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	6
	Coolant Pump Failure
	The I‑Pace uses liquid cooling for battery and motors — pump failure can cause reduced charging speeds or power limiting. A rare but known failure.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data
* "Interesting insight": The I‑Pace's most-enquired problem isn't the drive unit — it's the 12V battery. 15+ of the 40 enquiries relate to flat 12V batteries, not motor or battery pack failures. This is a manageable consumable issue, not a fundamental EV flaw. (Data point [JAG-VERIFIED].)
* The 2021 model year is a genuine dividing line — rear motor bearing enquiries drop off sharply on post-2021 cars, and infotainment complaints fall to near-zero. If you're buying used, 2021+ is the clear recommendation. 
* The I‑Pace has no internal combustion engine replacement data because it has no internal combustion engine. What we're seeing instead is a small but growing pattern of rear motor bearing enquiries on early cars — a known wear item that's worth checking before purchase.

	Labour estimate: Fitting an I‑Pace drive unit typically takes 8–14 hours per unit (10–14 hrs for rear, 8–12 hrs for front) at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [JAG-QUOTE]. Front and rear drive units are independent — failure of one does not necessarily mean the other needs replacement.
The I‑Pace rule — the squeeze is the story: Unlike the rest of the Jaguar range, the I‑Pace has no internal combustion engine to worry about. A complete dual-drive-unit replacement at £6,500–£9,000 sits at roughly 20–40% of a typical £18,000–£45,000 I‑Pace value — well below our 60% scrap threshold for Jaguar. But the real economics question isn't the drive unit — it's the battery. A battery replacement (not covered in this data) can exceed £15,000–£20,000, which pushes even a £35,000 car toward the threshold. The smart move? Buy a 2021+ car with a healthy battery health certificate and treat the 12V battery as a normal consumable. [THIRD-PARTY] — don't walk away from a drive unit repair; walk away from a car with poor battery health.

Buyer Profile
	Rating

	Family Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The I‑Pace has a spacious cabin and 505L boot — enough for a family of four. The ride is comfortable, but rear headroom is limited for tall passengers. A 2021+ car with Pivi Pro is the family choice — better tech for long journeys.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★★★
	230–290 miles real-world range (depending on 2018 vs 2021) makes the I‑Pace a credible commuter. The 2021+ car's improved battery management means 20+ more miles per charge than early cars. If you can charge at home, it's a brilliant daily driver.
	Budget Owner
	★★★☆☆
	A 2018–2020 car from £18k–£25k is excellent value, but factor in the battery health check (£150–£300) and 12V battery replacement (£200–£400) as immediate costs. The budget sweet spot is a 2019–2020 car with verified >88% SOH.
	Performance Buyer
	★★★★☆
	0–60 in 4.5 seconds and 394bhp across all years — the I‑Pace is genuinely quick. The 2021+ car's thermal management means you can enjoy that performance for longer without overheating. The Sportier EV choice over a Tesla Model Y.
	First-Time Premium Buyer
	★★★★½
	The I‑Pace is a gateway car — your first premium EV, your first Jaguar, or both. It's more engaging to drive than a Tesla, more prestigious than a Kia EV6, and the 2021 update fixes the early teething issues. Buy a 2021+ car and you'll have a genuinely good ownership experience.

SECTION 13 — I‑PACE FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: Jaguar I‑Pace Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does a Jaguar I‑Pace drive unit replacement cost in the UK?
A single rear drive unit costs £3,500–£5,000 reconditioned supply-only [JAG-QUOTE]; a complete dual-motor system is £6,500–£9,000 [JAG-QUOTE]. Labour adds £650–£1,300 depending on location. The 12V battery is a much cheaper £200–£400 fix. Use our diagnostic calculator →
Q2: Which I‑Pace drive unit is the most reliable?
The post-2021 rear motor and the front motor (all years) carry the cleanest record in our data. Early rear motors (2018–2020) have a documented bearing wear issue that was revised from 2021 onward. The 2021+ rear motor is the one to target.
Q3: Which I‑Pace should I avoid?
No model year to avoid outright, but pre-2021 cars without Pivi Pro come with a significant caveat — the Touch Pro Duo infotainment is slow and laggy, and early rear motors carry the bearing wear risk. A 2018–2020 car at a £5,000–£8,000 discount is still a good buy if you accept the trade-offs.
Q4: Is the I‑Pace battery degradation problem real?
All EV batteries degrade — it's normal. The I‑Pace's LG Chem 90kWh pack typically holds 85–95% state of health at 5–7 years, meaning 10–25 miles of range loss. Rapid degradation is rare and covered by the 8-year/100,000-mile battery warranty. Always ask for a battery health certificate before buying.
Q5: Is it worth replacing an I‑Pace drive unit?
Yes — the typical £6,500–£9,000 dual-motor replacement cost is well within the £18,000–£45,000 vehicle value (18–40% of value). This passes our 60% scrap threshold with room to spare. Battery replacement is the bigger economics question — not covered here, but a £15k–£20k battery on a £20k car is a tougher decision.
Q6: Which I‑Pace generation should I buy?
2021+ — the Pivi Pro infotainment upgrade and rear motor bearing revision make it the definitive I‑Pace. A 2021 car costs £5,000–£8,000 more than a 2018 example, but the reduced hassle and better residuals justify the premium. If budget is tight, a 2019–2020 car with verified battery health is a good second choice.
Q7: How do I find my I‑Pace drive unit code?
The drive unit code is stamped on the motor housing (visible from underneath) and is also listed in the vehicle's documentation under "motor type." For the I‑Pace, it's either E‑Drive Front (90kW) or E‑Drive Rear (90kW). If you're unsure, our specialists can identify it for you — reach out to our vetted network.
Q8: I‑Pace vs Tesla Model Y — which is more reliable?
The I‑Pace is more engaging to drive and has a more premium interior; the Model Y has better range, charging infrastructure, and tech. On reliability, the I‑Pace's drive units are generally robust (post-2021), but the 12V battery is a known weak point. Tesla's build quality is more variable — the I‑Pace is the more consistent ownership proposition, especially if you value driving dynamics over range. Read the full comparison →

The natural upgrade path is either a higher-spec I‑Pace (2021+ vs 2018–2020) or a switch to a Range Rover EV (future) for buyers wanting something larger.

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: F-Type — One Generation, Three Powertrain Eras
Sub-headline: The X152 ran from 2013 to 2024 with one chassis but three distinct engine families — choose by reliability as much as soundtrack.
________________


X152 (2013–2024) — The F-Type's only generation
Three distinct powertrain eras across a single chassis
2013–2019: Supercharged V6 & V8 Era
* AJ126 3.0 V6 SC (340–380bhp)
* AJ133 5.0 V8 SC (495–575bhp, SVR)
★ ★ ★ ☆ ☆ (pre-2019 cooling system caveats apply)
Our Verdict: The sound that made the F-Type famous. The AJ133 V8 is glorious — but pre-2019 cars need a cooling-system inspection before you buy, and the AJ126's plastic crossover pipes are a known weak point. Budget £1,000–£2,000 for cooling-system preventative work if history is unclear.
Explore X152 (2013–2019) →
________________


2019–2024: Revised V8 + Straight-Six Era
* AJ133 5.0 V8 SC (facelift revisions, 450–575bhp)
* AJ20D6 3.0 straight-six (P300/P340/P400)
★ ★ ★ ★ ★ (post-2019) / ★ ★ ★ ★ ☆ (revised V8)
Our Verdict: The car Jaguar should have built from the start. The AJ20D6 straight-six ditches the V6's cooling-system weak points for a clean reliability record, and the revised V8 carries over the 2013+ tensioner improvements. Buy a 2019+ car with the straight-six if reliability is your priority — you lose none of the character.
Explore X152 (2019–2024) →

Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? AJ126, AJ133 and AJ20D6 ratings: High confidence — based on 560+ [JAG-VERIFIED] enquiries across the AJ126 and AJ133 engine families, cross-referenced with specialist interviews and documented failure patterns. Data note (internal):
* Correction applied: The original data file listed 306DT (2.7/3.0 TDV6 diesel) as an F-Type engine. This was corrected → no diesel engine was ever fitted to the F-Type. Removed from engine list.
* Correction applied: 508PN was initially listed as an F-Type engine code. Corrected → 508PN is the naturally aspirated 5.0 V8 (used in XK, XJ). The F-Type uses AJ133 (supercharged 5.0 V8), not 508PN. 508PN has been removed from the F-Type engine list.
* AJ20D6 verification: Confirmed via live verification that the 3.0 straight-six Ingenium (AJ20D6) was introduced to the F-Type in 2019 (facelift) as the replacement for the AJ126 V6. Power outputs: P300 (300bhp), P340 (340bhp), P400 (400bhp). This is correctly attributed as the F-Type's late-generation engine.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common F-Type Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most frequent F-Type failure patterns — what to check, which engines they affect, and what they cost.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	AJ126 Cooling System Crossover Pipe Failure
	The plastic coolant crossover pipes on the supercharged 3.0 V6 crack with age and heat cycling. These engines cannot tolerate overheating without warping heads or damaging the supercharger. Pre-2016 cars are highest risk.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	2
	AJ133 Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (Pre-2013)
	The 5.0 V8's early-build weak point — chain tensioner design was revised in 2013. F-Type production started in 2013, so most F-Types are post-revision, but early 2013 cars without documented tensioner history need inspection.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	Supercharger Coupler / Torsion Isolator Rattle
	A well-known shared quirk of the AJ126 and AJ133 — the coupler between the supercharger and drive pulley wears, producing a rattling noise on idle. Rarely catastrophic on its own, but replacement involves supercharger removal.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	4
	AJ126 Water Pump Failure
	The water pump on the supercharged V6 has a documented failure pattern — leaks or seizes without warning. Pre-2016 cars are most affected. Replacement cost: £600–£1,200 [JAG-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	AJ133 Cooling System Crossover Pipe Leaks
	Same plastic crossover pipe weakness as the AJ126, but on the 5.0 V8. Later (2016+) cars use revised parts. Inspect for coolant residue around the engine valley.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	6
	ZF 8-Speed Gearbox Mechatronic Issues
	Shared across the JLR range — the 8HP's mechatronic unit can develop solenoid faults, causing harsh shifts or transmission faults. Not engine-specific but frequently enquired alongside engine problems.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	________________


Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
________________

Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The F-Type's 53 enquiries in 2025 make it Jaguar's lowest-volume model in the dataset — but its enquiry-to-value ratio is among the highest, with average host values of £18,000–£65,000 making replacement economics unusually safe compared to lower-value Jags. 
* The V6-to-straight-six transition is barely visible in the data. F-Type enquiries are overwhelmingly for the AJ126 V6 (560 across all models) — but this figure is aggregated across all AJ126-equipped Jags, not F-Type-specific. The AJ20D6 straight-six is too recent and too low-volume in F-Type fitment to generate meaningful proprietary data yet. 
* The F-Type's cooling-system failure pattern dominates the conversation. Across AJ126 and AJ133 enquiries, cooling system (crossover pipes, water pumps) and timing-chain tensioner (pre-2013 units) are the most frequently mentioned symptoms. A buyer of any pre-2019 F-Type should budget £1,000–£2,000 for cooling-system preventative work unless full history shows it's been done.
________________


SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every F-Type Buyer
"Buy a 2019+ car with the AJ20D6 straight-six if you want the cleanest reliability record — it trades the V6's cooling-system worries for a modern turbocharged six with no systemic failure patterns. If you must have the V8, buy a 2016+ car with documented cooling-system history and budget £1,500–£2,000 for preventative work. The V6 sounds glorious, but pre-2019 cooling system maintenance is a 'when', not an 'if'."
________________

Labour estimate: Fitting an F-Type engine typically takes 12–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [JAG-QUOTE].
________________


The F-Type rule — economics vs soundtrack: An F-Type's typical value of £18,000–£65,000 means even the AJ133's upper reconditioned cost of £9,000 sits at 15–50% of vehicle value — well below our 60% threshold for Jaguar. The F-Type is one of the safest Jaguars to replace an engine on, purely because its retained values comfortably absorb the cost. The only caveat: if your F-Type is a high-mileage, early-register car worth £10,000–£15,000, a £9,000 AJ133 rebuild starts to approach 60–90% of vehicle value. In that scenario, evaluate the car's condition, history and mileage before committing — a low-mileage example is always worth saving; a tired one might not be.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE F-TYPE ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the F-Type Engine Range Evolved — 2013 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Jaguar Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Launch Era — Supercharged V6 & V8
	2013–2015
	AJ126 3.0 V6 SC (340/380), AJ133 5.0 V8 SC (450/550), SVR (575)
	Jaguar needed a sports car to follow the XK — the F-Type launched with the AJ-V8 family already well-established from the XK and XJ.
	The AJ126 is essentially an AJ133 V8 with two cylinders removed — same architecture, same supercharger layout, same cooling-system design.
	Facelift Era — Straight-Six Arrives
	2019–2020
	AJ20D6 3.0 I6 Turbo (P300/P340/P400), AJ133 5.0 V8 SC (revised)
	JLR's Ingenium straight-six replaced the AJ126 V6 across the range — emissions, efficiency, and a clean-sheet design with no cooling-system weaknesses.
	The AJ20D6 is the same engine family as the Defender's D300 and Range Rover's P400 — a modular straight-six with no systemic failure patterns.
	Final Era — Electrification Prep
	2020–2024
	AJ20D6, AJ133 V8
	Jaguar's transition to all-electric by 2025 meant the F-Type's days were numbered — the V8 carried on in reduced volumes for enthusiasts.
	The F-Type was discontinued in 2024 with no direct successor — the last of Jaguar's internal-combustion sports cars.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY AN F-TYPE? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy an F-Type?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Performance Buyer
	★★★★★
	The F-Type's supercharged V8 delivers one of the best sports-car soundtracks of any modern car. The 575bhp SVR is the one to hunt — but budget £1,500–£2,000 for cooling-system preventative work on any pre-2019 car.
	Weekend Enthusiast
	★★★★☆
	The F-Type is at its best on a Sunday B-road. The V6 is sweeter than the V8 for pure handling balance — the 380bhp V6 is the enthusiast's sweet spot if you can live with the cooling-system caveats.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★☆☆
	The F-Type is a sports car, not a grand tourer. The ride is firm, the boot is small, and the running costs are real. If you're commuting daily in an F-Type, buy a 2019+ car with the AJ20D6 — it's the most civilised and the most reliable.
	Collector / Keeper
	★★★★☆
	The F-Type is the last of Jaguar's internal-combustion sports cars — that alone makes it a future classic. A low-mileage, early-register V8 SVR with full history is the long-term keeper. The AJ20D6 cars are better to own and drive, but the V8 will be the collector's choice.
	First-Time Sports Car Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The F-Type is a surprisingly approachable sports car — the V6 is manageable, the chassis is forgiving, and the drama is instantly addictive. Buy a 2019+ car with the AJ20D6: no cooling-system worries, modern infotainment, and the cleanest reliability record in the range.
	________________


SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My F-Type Engine — or Should I Replace It?
The F-Type's AJ126 V6 cooling-system failures are the most common repair — if the plastic crossover pipes have cracked and you've caught it early, a repair is usually viable. If it has overheated and warped the heads, replacement is often more cost-effective. The AJ133 V8 follows the same logic — cooling system repairs are routine, but a full rebuild is serious money.
Path 1 — Know your engine? Launch the Jaguar calculator with F-Type pre-selected → Start Diagnosis →
Path 2 — Not sure which engine you have? We'll identify it first — check your V5C or the engine block stamping, or use our identification tool → Identify My Engine →
________________

* 📊 Built on Real Data — Every reliability rating and cost range for the F-Type comes from genuine UK quote and enquiry data — 50+ F-Type enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED], cross-referenced against 560 AJ126 and 330 AJ133 enquiries across the Jaguar range — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork.
* 💚 We Tell You When Not to Repair — The F-Type is a rare Jaguar where replacement almost always makes sense — the car's value comfortably absorbs even the AJ133's upper reconditioned cost of £9,000, sitting at 15–50% of vehicle value. We only flag caution when a high-mileage early car's value drops below £15,000 and the repair cost approaches 60%.
* 🔧 Vetted Jaguar Specialists — Our network of 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists includes technicians who work on F-Types daily — they know the AJ126's cooling-system weak points, the AJ133's chain-tensioner history, and the AJ20D6's modern engineering.

SECTION 13 — F-TYPE FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: F-Type Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does an F-Type engine replacement cost in the UK?
An F-Type engine replacement typically costs £4,500–£9,000 for a reconditioned engine, supply-only [JAG-QUOTE]. Labour adds 12–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The AJ126 V6 sits at the lower end (£4,500–£7,500), while the AJ133 V8 sits higher (£5,500–£9,000). The AJ20D6 straight-six sits in the middle (£4,500–£7,500). Use our diagnostic calculator above to get a personalised quote for your specific car.
Q2: Which F-Type engine is the most reliable?
The AJ20D6 3.0 straight-six (fitted to 2019+ P300/P340/P400 cars) is the most reliable F-Type engine. It has no systemic cooling-system weaknesses, no chain-tensioner caveats, and no supercharger-coupler wear issues — it's a clean-sheet design that has proven robust across the JLR range. Read our AJ20D6 Knowledge Centre → for the full breakdown.
Q3: Which F-Type engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but the AJ126 3.0 V6 SC (2013–2019) carries a real caveat: pre-2019 cars have documented cooling-system crossover pipe failures. If you're buying an early V6, insist on evidence that the cooling system has been inspected and the pipes replaced if needed. The AJ133 5.0 V8 is robust post-2013, but cooling-system maintenance is still a "when", not an "if".
Q4: Is the F-Type timing chain problem real?
The timing chain tensioner issue affects the AJ133 5.0 V8 built before 2013 — the design was revised in 2013 with a marked reliability improvement. F-Type production started in 2013, so most F-Types are post-revision, but early 2013 cars without documented chain-tensioner history warrant an inspection. Symptoms include a rattle on cold start — if you hear it, have a specialist check it immediately.
Q5: Is it worth replacing an F-Type engine?
Yes — almost always. The F-Type's typical value of £18,000–£65,000 means even the AJ133's upper reconditioned cost of £9,000 sits at 15–50% of vehicle value, well below our 60% threshold for Jaguar. The only exception is a high-mileage, early-register car worth £10,000–£15,000 where a £9,000 V8 rebuild starts to approach 60–90% of value. In that scenario, evaluate the car's condition and history before committing.
Q6: Which F-Type year should I buy?
Buy a 2019+ car with the AJ20D6 straight-six if your priority is reliability and modern engineering. Buy a 2013–2019 V6 if the supercharged soundtrack is non-negotiable — but budget £1,000–£2,000 for cooling-system preventative work unless history shows it's been done. Buy the V8 if you want the full-fat experience — the post-2013 tensioner revisions make it robust, but cooling-system maintenance is essential.
Q7: How do I find my F-Type engine code?
Your F-Type's engine code is on the VIN plate (character 4–8), stamped on the engine block (passenger side, rear), and on the V5C registration document. The codes are: AJ126 (3.0 V6 SC, 2013–2019), AJ133 (5.0 V8 SC, 2013–2024), and AJ20D6 (3.0 straight-six, 2019–2024). If you're unsure, use our identification tool → Identify My Engine →
Q8: F-Type vs XK — which is more reliable?
The F-Type's AJ133 V8 is the direct descendant of the XK's AJ133 — the F-Type benefits from the 2013+ timing-chain revisions that transformed the V8's reliability record. The XK carries the pre-2013 risk on its 5.0 cars. For V6 buyers, the F-Type's AJ126 is the same engine as the XK's later V6 — identical cooling-system caveats. The F-Type wins on reliability purely because of age: it's a newer car with more recent engineering. Read our full F-Type vs XK comparison →
________________

Succession/upgrade funnel:
The F-Type was discontinued in 2024 with no direct successor. Jaguar's transition to all-electric by 2025 means the F-Type is the last of Jaguar's internal-combustion sports cars — its natural successor is the planned electric GT (2025+), but for now, the F-Type is the end of the line for petrol-powered Jaguar sports cars. If you're looking for a more practical Jaguar, consider the F-Pace → or XF →.

Classification: Premium Style / Widest Economics Swing

★★★★★
Our Verdict: The definitive XK — Ian Callum's aluminium masterpiece. The 2009 facelift brought the 5.0 V8 with much-improved timing chain design. This is the one to buy, especially the naturally aspirated 5.0 Portfolio or the thunderous XKR-S.

________________


The 2009 Facelift — The Dividing Line
Pre-2009 (4.2-litre): The original X150 launched in 2006 with the 4.2 AJ34 V8 — a proven unit, but the 5.0 that replaced it is objectively better in every way.
2009–2014 (5.0-litre): The 5.0 AJ133 brought more power (385bhp naturally aspirated, 503–542bhp supercharged), a revised timing chain tensioner design, and a subtle styling refresh. This is the generation every informed buyer targets.
Read the full comparison: X100 vs X150 →

Data note (internal):
Correction applied: The source data file lists "508PS" as an engine code for the XK. 508PS is the marketing power designation for the 5.0 supercharged V8; the factory engine code is AJ133 (with supercharged variants carrying the same base code). The home Jaguar homepage correctly identifies AJ133 / 508PN as the 5.0 V8 family.
Correction applied: The source data file groups all AJ133 enquiries (330) under a single code. This figure includes XF and XJ enquiries as well as XK — it is not XK-exclusive. The XK-specific enquiry volume for 5.0-litre cars is lower; the 330 figure is cited in the engine table as the total for the engine family, with the model-level XK figure (120 for X100 + approx 170 for X150 = 290+) used in the hero and trust strip.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common XK Engine Problems
Sub-headline: What actually fails on the XK's AJ-V8 engines — and what it costs to fix.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	AJ27 4.0 V8 Bore Wear
	Early X100 engines (pre-2000) can suffer cylinder bore wear, causing misfires and blue exhaust smoke on throttle. Full engine rebuild is the only permanent fix.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	2
	AJ-V8 Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	Pre-2009 AJ27 and early AJ34 engines have a documented timing chain tensioner weakness. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — catastrophic if ignored.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	AJ133 5.0 V8 Water Pump Failure
	The 5.0 V8's water pump is a known weak point across the AJ133 family. Owner reports confirm it's a routine replacement item — catch it before overheating causes head-gasket damage.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	4
	ZF 6-Speed Gearbox Torque Converter
	Shared across the X150 range — torque converter lock-up clutch failure causes gearbox slippage, grinding noises and transmission warning lights.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	Battery Drain & Electrical Gremlins
	XKs are sensitive to battery condition — modules failing to sleep correctly is a frequent no-start complaint. Always use a premium battery.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	6
	Fuel Injector Failure (5.0 V8)
	Faulty injectors on the 5.0 AJ133 cause rough running, reduced power, stalling and excessive fuel consumption.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
________________

	Insights from the data
* Interesting insight: The X150 generation accounts for roughly 60% of all XK enquiries in 2025 — the aluminium grand tourer is the one buyers are actively researching, while the X100 is increasingly the preserve of collectors and enthusiasts 
* The 5.0 AJ133 engine (fitted to 2009–2014 X150s) generates significantly more enquiry volume than any other XK engine — buyers are actively seeking the post-facelift car, and specialists report this is the generation with the strongest reliability record 
* Early 4.0 X100 cars generate relatively low enquiry volume but high forum activity — the enthusiast community is active, but the cars are now cheap enough that a full engine replacement often fails the economics threshold for all but the best examples

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every XK Buyer
Buy a 2009–2014 X150 with the 5.0 naturally aspirated V8, full service history, and evidence the water pump has been replaced. It's the sweet spot — the most reliable engine, the best-looking generation, and values that are still £10,000–£20,000 below where they should be.

________________


The XK rule — the value swing is the story: The XK's value range is one of the widest in the Jaguar line-up. An early X100 XK8 worth £4,000–£5,000 facing a £5,000+ reconditioned engine sits at 100%+ of vehicle value — well above our 60% scrap threshold for Jaguar. The same engine in a 2014 XKR-S worth £30,000–£35,000 sits at just 20–30% of vehicle value — a no-brainer replacement. Before committing to a recon engine, get a realistic market valuation for your specific car and run the numbers. For X100s, used engines often make more sense; for X150s, a reconditioned unit is usually the right call.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE XK ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the XK Engine Range Evolved — 1996 to 2014
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Jaguar Changed
	Worth Knowing
	The AJ27 Era
	1996–2002
	AJ27 4.0 V8, AJ27S 4.0 Supercharged
	The original XK8 launched with Jaguar's new 4.0-litre V8 — a clean-sheet design replacing the straight-six XJS.
	Early cars (pre-2000) have a reputation for bore wear — always inspect before buying.
	The 4.2 Upgrade
	2002–2006
	AJ34 4.2 V8, AJ34S 4.2 Supercharged
	Jaguar increased displacement to 4.2 litres in 2002, bringing more power and improved reliability — the bore-wear issues were largely resolved.
	The 4.2 is significantly more robust than the early 4.0 — this is the X100 to target.
	The Aluminium Revolution
	2006–2009
	AJ34 4.2 V8, AJ34S 4.2 Supercharged
	The X150 launched with an aluminium monocoque, losing 150kg over the X100 — the 4.2 engine carried over initially.
	The X150 chassis is a masterpiece — Ian Callum's finest work.
	The 5.0 Transformation
	2009–2014
	AJ133 5.0 V8, AJ133S 5.0 Supercharged
	The 2009 facelift brought the 5.0-litre AJ133 V8 with 385bhp (naturally aspirated) and up to 542bhp (supercharged) — plus a revised timing chain tensioner design.
	This is the definitive XK engine — more power, stronger reliability, and the one specialists recommend.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY AN XK? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy an XK?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Performance Buyer
	★★★★★
	The XKR-S is one of the most underrated performance cars on the used market — 542bhp, 200mph+, and values that haven't yet caught up to the driving experience. Buy a 2012+ car with full service history.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★☆☆
	The XK is a genuine GT — comfortable, refined, and capable of daily use. But be realistic: fuel economy is 18–25mpg, and maintenance costs reflect the original £70,000 price tag. A 2009+ 5.0 naturally aspirated is the sweet spot.
	Family Buyer
	★★☆☆☆
	The XK is a 2+2 at best — rear seats are tiny, and even children will complain. This is a car for couples, not families. If you need practicality, look at the F-Pace instead.
	Budget Owner
	★★☆☆☆
	An early X100 can be bought for under £5,000 — but a £5,000 engine replacement will quickly exceed the car's value. Buy the best X150 5.0 you can afford, not the cheapest X100 you can find.
	Collector / Keeper
	★★★★★
	The XK, especially the X150, is a future classic. Low-mileage examples with full service history are already appreciating. The XKR-S and Portfolio models are the ones to hold long-term.
	________________

SECTION 13 — XK FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: XK Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does an XK engine replacement cost in the UK?
An XK engine replacement costs between £3,000 and £10,000 supply-only depending on engine choice — the 4.0 AJ27 sits at the lower end (£3,000–£5,500), while the 5.0 AJ133 supercharged sits at the top (£6,500–£10,000) [JAG-QUOTE]. Labour adds approximately 12–18 hours at £65–£95/hr for an independent specialist. Use our diagnostic calculator to get a personalised estimate for your specific XK.
Q2: Which XK engine is the most reliable?
The AJ133 5.0 V8 (2009–2014) is the most reliable XK engine. The 2009 facelift brought a revised timing chain tensioner design that resolved the weakness of earlier AJ-V8s, and the 5.0-litre unit has proven robust across the X150 range. The naturally aspirated 385bhp version is the sweet spot — simpler than the supercharged variant with fewer potential failure points.
Q3: Which XK engine should I avoid?
Avoid the early AJ27 4.0 V8 (1996–2000) without documented history — these engines suffer from bore wear (blue smoke on throttle, misfires) and timing chain tensioner issues. If you're set on an X100, buy a 2003+ 4.2 car — the AJ34 is significantly more robust. For the X150, all engines are solid, but prioritise a 2009+ 5.0 car for the best reliability.
Q4: Does the XK have timing chain problems?
Yes — pre-2009 AJ-V8 engines (AJ27, early AJ34) have a documented timing chain tensioner weakness. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign. If you hear it, book an inspection immediately — ignoring it can cause catastrophic engine failure. Post-2009 5.0 AJ133 engines have a revised design and are much more robust.
Q5: Is it worth replacing an XK engine?
It depends entirely on the car's value. A 2014 XKR-S worth £30,000–£35,000 with a £6,500–£10,000 recon cost sits at 20–30% of vehicle value — absolutely worth replacing. A 1998 XK8 worth £4,000–£5,000 facing a £5,000+ recon cost sits at over 100% of vehicle value — not worth it. If replacement cost exceeds roughly 60% of your car's market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
Q6: Which XK should I buy — X100 or X150?
Buy an X150 (2009–2014) with the 5.0 naturally aspirated V8 — it's the best-looking generation, the most reliable engine, and values are still £10,000–£20,000, which is remarkably low for a car of this quality. The X100 is a beautiful classic but the economics are tighter — only buy one if you're prepared for classic-car ownership costs.
Q7: How do I find my XK engine code?
Your XK's engine code can be found in three places: (1) on the VIN plate inside the driver's door sill — the engine code is part of the VIN; (2) on the engine block itself — stamped on the front of the AJ-V8 block; (3) in your service documentation or V5C logbook. Our 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists can also identify your engine if you're unsure.
Q8: XK vs XKR — which is more reliable?
Both use the same AJ-V8 engine family — the XKR simply adds a supercharger. The naturally aspirated XK has fewer components to fail (no supercharger, no intercooler), so it's technically the more reliable option. However, the XKR's supercharged V8 is robust when maintained — 2009+ examples with the 5.0 AJ133S have proven very reliable. Our honest advice: buy the XKR if you want the performance; the reliability difference is minor. Read the full comparison: XK vs XKR →

A-Class
Classification: First-Time Buyer / Gateway
Justification: The A-Class is Mercedes-Benz's high-volume entry-level model, often a buyer's first Mercedes. The defining line is the 2018+ W177 generation, which moved to the M282 petrol and OM608 diesel engines—a clear hardware break from the W176's M270/OM651 era.
________________

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: A-Class Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Four generations, four very different cars. The W168 and W169 are run-out classics, the W176 is the budget-performance sweet spot, and the W177 is the modern Mercedes.
W168 (1997–2004)
1997–2004 • M166 petrol • OM668 diesel
★★☆☆☆
Our Verdict: The original A-Class—innovative sandwich-floor platform, but engine reliability is a gamble on 25-year-old cars. Only buy as a classic project with full history. Recon cost frequently exceeds vehicle value.
Explore W168 →
W169 (2004–2012)
2004–2012 • M266 petrol • OM640 diesel
★★☆☆☆
Our Verdict: The second generation improved cabin quality but inherited the W168's high centre of gravity. OM640 diesel has injector and EGR issues; M266 petrol is the safer bet. Most examples now sub-£4k—replacement rarely makes economic sense.
Explore W169 →
W176 (2012–2018)
2012–2018 • M270 petrol • OM651 diesel • OM607 diesel
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The generation that finally made the A-Class a proper premium hatch. The M270 petrol is the reliable choice; the OM651 diesel carries "Black Death" injector seal risk but is well-understood. The sweet spot is the 2015+ facelift.
Explore W176 →
W177 (2018–present) 🔥 Most Enquired — 122 enquiries (2025) [MB-VERIFIED]
2018–present • M282 petrol • M260 petrol • M139 petrol (AMG) • OM608 diesel • OM654 diesel
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The modern A-Class—proper Mercedes quality, excellent M282 petrol and OM608 diesel engines, and the first A-Class with genuinely good dynamics. The facelift (2022+) with OM654 diesel is the safest A-Class ever built.
Explore W177 →
________________


The A-Class dividing line: the 2018 revolution
The W177 (2018) is the generation that finally made the A-Class a convincing Mercedes. It moved to the M282 1.3-litre petrol (co-developed with Renault) and the OM608 1.5-litre diesel (Renault K9K-based)—both significantly more reliable than the OM651 it replaced. The W176's OM651 diesel carries the same "Black Death" injector seal risk as the C-Class and E-Class; the W177's OM608 is a much cleaner design. Buy post-2018 if your budget allows.
Read the full comparison: A-Class W176 vs W177 →
________________


Badge-ambiguity check: The "A 180" badge covers at least three completely different engines depending on generation and year:
* W169 A 180 = M266 1.7-litre petrol
* W176 A 180 CDI = OM607 1.5-litre diesel (early) or OM651 2.2-litre diesel (later)
* W177 A 180 = M282 1.3-litre petrol
Always check the engine code on the VIN plate before buying—the badge tells you almost nothing about what's under the bonnet.
________________

Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? OM651 and OM654 ratings: high confidence — based on 1,250 and 520 enquiries respectively [MB-VERIFIED], plus cross-referencing with specialist interviews and manufacturer TSBs. W168/W169 engine ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist knowledge, not proprietary volume data, as these generations predate the enquiry dataset.
Data note (internal):
Correction applied: The original sheet listed OM651 as a W169 engine; verification shows OM651 was not fitted to the W169—it launched with the W176 in 2012. Corrected. The W169's diesel is OM640.
Correction applied: The W176's "A 180 CDI" was originally listed with OM651 only; verification shows early W176 A 180 CDI (2012–2014) used OM607 1.5-litre diesel, with OM651 appearing later. Split accordingly.
No further corrections needed—source data matched live verification for all other entries.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common A-Class Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns reported by UK A-Class owners—from the W168 to the W177.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	OM651 "Black Death" Injector Seal Failure
	The defining Mercedes diesel issue and our #1 enquiry driver across the A-Class range. Copper injector seals degrade, leaking combustion gases into a carbon "tar" that can weld the injector into the head if ignored. Most common on W176 A 200 CDI / A 220 CDI (2012–2018). £400–£1,200 seal repair; up to full engine replacement if neglected [MB-QUOTE].
	🔴 Catastrophic if Ignored
	Read more →
	2
	OM651 Rear Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	The OM651's timing chain sits at the transmission end—access requires separating the engine from the gearbox. W176 A 200 CDI / A 220 CDI affected. Chain replacement: £600–£1,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Early warning: rattle on cold start.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	3
	OM640 Injector and EGR Issues
	W169 OM640 diesel suffers from injector seal leaks and EGR valve clogging—a precursor to the "Black Death" issues that became notorious on the later OM651. £300–£800 injector repair; £400–£1,200 EGR replacement [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	4
	M270 Timing Chain Stretch
	W176 M270 petrol (2012–2018) can suffer from timing chain stretch on higher-mileage examples—less common than the diesel OM651 but still a known weak point. Chain replacement: £1,500–£2,500 [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	W168/W169 Sandwich-Floor Specific Issues
	The first-generation A-Class's unique sandwich-floor design makes engine access labour-intensive. M166 petrol engines can suffer from head gasket failure; OM668 diesels have injector and turbo issues on high-mileage examples. Both generations now 20+ years old—most repairs exceed vehicle value.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	6
	Electrical & Sensor Failures (All Generations)
	A-Class models across all generations report sensor failures (crankshaft position, camshaft position, MAF) and electrical gremlins—often misdiagnosed as engine mechanical failure. A £150–£400 sensor replacement can save a £2,500+ engine replacement [MB-QUOTE].
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: A-Class Market Intelligence — What 750+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	Most-enquired A-Class generation
	W177 — 122 enquiries [MB-VERIFIED]
	⬆ Rising
	Most-enquired A-Class engine (all models)
	OM651 — 1,250 enquiries [MB-VERIFIED]
	➡ Stable
	Petrol vs diesel split
	~60% diesel, ~40% petrol [MB-VERIFIED]
	⬆ Petrol rising
	Insights from the data
* Interesting insight: The W177 (2018+) has overtaken the W176 as the most-enquired A-Class generation in our 2025 data—122 enquiries vs the W176's cohort. This suggests the W177 is now entering the "used buyer sweet spot" where owners are researching engine reliability and replacement costs before purchase. 
* The OM651 diesel—despite its "Black Death" reputation—remains the most-enquired engine across all A-Class models. This reflects the sheer volume of OM651-powered W176 A 200 CDI and A 220 CDI units on UK roads, not an increase in failure rate. The engine is well-understood and repairable if caught early. 
* The W168 and W169 generations barely register in our 2025 enquiry data—they're now classic-car territory. Most owners of these generations are enthusiasts or budget buyers who would scrap rather than replace an engine. The advice for these cars is simple: enjoy them while they run, but don't spend £2,500+ on an engine replacement for a £1,500 car. 
________________


SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every A-Class Buyer
If you're buying a used A-Class, prioritise the W177 (2018+) with the M282 petrol or OM608 diesel. The W176 is a good budget buy—but only with the M270 petrol. Walk away from any W176 diesel without documented injector service history, and treat the W168/W169 as classic projects, not daily drivers.

Labour estimate: Fitting an A‑Class engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. Labour for AMG variants (M133/M139) may run to 16 hours due to additional cooling and performance‑system disassembly.
________________


The A‑Class rule — generation is everything:
A W176 OM651 diesel recon at £2,800–£4,800 against a typical W176 value of £5,000–£14,000 sits at around 30–70% of the car's worth. On a low‑value W176 (sub‑£6,000), that pushes past our 70% Mercedes threshold — so run the numbers carefully. For a W177 with OM654, the same £3,000–£5,000 recon against an £18,000–£35,000 car is comfortably below 30% — a clear fix it verdict. For W168/W169 cars worth £1,500–£5,000, any engine replacement is almost always a walk away decision. Always compare the lowest viable recon cost against your car's current market value — if it exceeds 70%, get a scrap valuation before committing.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE A‑CLASS ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the A‑Class Engine Range Evolved — 1997 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	W168 – the original
	1997–2004
	M166 (petrol), OM668 (diesel)
	Mercedes needed a compact city car to compete with the VW Golf and Audi A3. The sandwich‑floor design was revolutionary for safety but made engine access tricky.
	The OM668 diesel was one of Mercedes' first common‑rail diesels — reliable when maintained, but parts are now scarce and expensive.
	W169 – refinement
	2004–2012
	M266 (petrol), OM640 (diesel)
	The W169 was a complete rethink — a more conventional body style, better packaging, and a switch to the M266 petrol (an improved version of the M166) and OM640 diesel (predecessor to the OM651).
	This generation introduced the "A 180 CDI" badge that confused buyers for years — it used the OM640, not the later OM651.
	W176 – the premium hatch
	2012–2018
	M270 (petrol), OM651/OM607 (diesel), M133 (AMG)
	Mercedes shifted the A‑Class from a tall MPV‑like shape to a proper premium hatchback to take on the BMW 1 Series and Audi A3. This meant a new engine layout — front‑mounted longitudinal? No, actually transverse, but with a much sportier chassis.
	The OM651 diesel fitted here carries the same "Black Death" injector seal risk as the C‑Class and E‑Class — but the M270 petrol is one of the most reliable engines Mercedes built in this era.
	W177 – the modern era
	2018–present
	M282 (petrol), M260/M139 (petrol), OM608/OM654 (diesel)
	Emissions regulations (Euro 6d) forced Mercedes to downsize and modularise. The M282 1.3‑litre petrol (co‑developed with Renault) replaced the M270, and the OM608 1.5‑litre diesel (Renault K9K‑based) replaced the OM607 and early OM651. The OM654 arrived with the 2022 facelift.
	The OM654 is the cleanest diesel ever fitted to an A‑Class — it's also used in the C‑Class and E‑Class, so parts and expertise are widely available. The M139 in the A 45 S AMG is the most powerful four‑cylinder production engine in the world (416 hp).
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY AN A‑CLASS? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy an A‑Class?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	★★★☆☆
	The A‑Class is a practical premium hatch — the W177 offers decent rear legroom and a 355‑litre boot, but the CLA or C‑Class are better if you regularly carry three passengers. Choose a W177 with M282 petrol for low running costs.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★★☆
	The A‑Class is an excellent commuter — the W177's M282 petrol or OM608 diesel are frugal and refined, while the W176's M270 petrol is a reliable choice. Avoid W176 OM651 diesels with no injector history.
	Budget Owner
	★★☆☆☆
	The W169 and W168 are cheap to buy but expensive to keep — repairs can quickly exceed the car's value. A W176 M270 petrol is the best budget pick (£4,000–£8,000) if you can find one with a documented service history.
	Performance Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The A 45 AMG (W176 or W177) is a hot‑hatch legend — the M133 and M139 engines are robust, but they demand premium maintenance. The W177 A 35 AMG is a more affordable entry point with the M260 engine.
	First‑Time Premium Buyer
	★★★★★
	The A‑Class is the gateway to Mercedes ownership — the W177 is the perfect entry point: modern, stylish, and with the most reliable engine lineup in A‑Class history. If budget is tight, the W176 M270 petrol is a solid alternative, but avoid the OM651 diesel without full service records.
	________________

SECTION 13 — A‑CLASS FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: A‑Class Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does an A‑Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
A full A‑Class engine replacement costs £2,800–£5,000 for a reconditioned unit (supply‑only) [MB-QUOTE], with labour adding £520–£780 (independent) or more in London/SE. The exact figure depends on your engine code: OM651 and OM654 are the most common, while AMG engines (M133/M139) can cost £8,000–£14,000 for a recon supply. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised quote based on your engine and vehicle value.
Q2: Which A‑Class engine is the most reliable?
The OM654 diesel (fitted to the W177 facelift from 2022) is the most reliable A‑Class engine — it has materially fewer failure reports than the OM651 and a much cleaner emissions design. Among petrols, the M270 (W176) and M282 (W177) are both robust, with the M282 being especially reliable due to its simpler turbocharged design.
Q3: Which A‑Class engine should I avoid?
We don't say "avoid" outright, but the OM651 diesel (W176, 2012–2018) carries a real caveat: "Black Death" injector seal failure and rear‑timing‑chain complexity. It's a manageable, preventable issue with regular servicing — but buy only with documented injector service history. We also advise against any W168/W169 engine if you're looking for a daily driver — parts are scarce and replacement costs often exceed vehicle value.
Q4: Is the A‑Class timing chain problem real?
Yes — the OM651 diesel's timing chain sits at the gearbox end, making replacement labour‑intensive (8–12 hours). Early warning signs are a rattle on cold start and erratic idle. On the M270 petrol, timing chain stretch is less common but can occur on high‑mileage examples. If you hear a rattle, have it inspected immediately — chain replacement costs £600–£1,500, but a snapped chain can write off the engine.
Q5: Is it worth replacing an A‑Class engine?
It depends on your generation. For a W177 (£18,000–£35,000 value), a £3,000–£5,000 recon is well within the 70% threshold — worth it. For a W176 (£5,000–£14,000), an OM651 recon at £2,800–£4,800 can exceed 70% on lower‑value examples — run the numbers. For a W168/W169 (under £5,000), a replacement almost never makes financial sense — scrap it unless you're a classic enthusiast.
Q6: Which A‑Class generation should I buy?
The W177 (2018+) is the unequivocal recommendation — it's the safest, most reliable, and best‑value used buy. If your budget is tighter, the W176 (2015+ facelift) with the M270 petrol is a solid alternative — avoid pre‑2015 OM651 diesels unless you have full service records. Avoid the W168 and W169 unless you're buying a classic project.
Q7: How do I find my A‑Class engine code?
The easiest way is to check your VIN plate (visible through the windscreen, or on the driver's door pillar). The engine code is characters 6–8 of the VIN (e.g. "OM651" or "M270"). You can also find it on the engine block itself — on most A‑Classes, it's stamped on the front of the cylinder head or on a sticker near the timing cover. If you're unsure, use our identification tool in the calculator section above.
Q8: A‑Class vs CLA — which is more reliable?
They share engines — the CLA is essentially a four‑door coupe version of the A‑Class, so reliability is identical for the same powertrain. The difference is styling and practicality: the CLA offers a sleeker profile but less rear headroom and boot space. If you value space, buy the A‑Class; if you want style, buy the CLA — but for reliability, both are equal. Read the full comparison: A‑Class vs CLA →
________________

DATA INTEGRITY NOTES (internal — read before publishing)
1. Schema Block 9 (AggregateRating) has been omitted — this page does not yet have a genuine, verifiable review count or rating score for MercedesEngine.uk. If and when real review data exists, it should be added as a nested aggregateRating property under the Organization or Service block, with both ratingValue and reviewCount populated from the source data.
2. Car schema has been added — the EngineSpecification block (Block 8) represents the vehicle's engine specifications, which is a genuinely useful addition for AI answer engines. The Product block (Block 9) represents the engine replacement service itself, carrying the commercial intent Google needs to understand this is a transaction-oriented page.
3. The lowPrice and highPrice values in the Service and Product blocks are pulled directly from Section 8's real cost tables — used £1,500 (the lowest OM651 used price) and £5,000 (the highest OM654 recon price). These reflect the genuine range for A-Class engine replacements in the UK.
4. Placeholder costs for W168/W169, M282, OM608 and AMG engines are deliberately omitted from the schema pricing — only the OM651 and OM654 cost ranges have [MB-QUOTE] backing. When those other engine cost ranges are sourced and verified, the lowPrice/highPrice range should be updated to reflect the full spectrum of A-Class engine replacement costs.
5. The datePublished and dateModified fields are set to today's date (2026-07-07). These should be updated each time the page content is substantially revised — the dateModified field is especially important for E-E-A-T signals.

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: B-Class Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: The B-Class runs in two distinct generations — the earlier W245 (2005–2011) and the more modern W246 (2011–2018). The dividing line is sharp.
________________


W245 B-Class (2005–2011)
Years: 2005–2011 • Key Engines: OM640 2.0 CDI, M266 1.5/2.0 petrol, M270 1.6 turbo petrol
★★☆☆☆
Our Verdict: The first-generation B-Class is a spacious, practical MPV, but its engines are now old enough that replacement cost can exceed vehicle value. The OM640 diesel in particular carries a watch‑and‑check caveat — injector and EGR failures are common, and a full recon at £2,800–£4,800 can easily exceed the sub‑£4k W245 market value [MB‑QUOTE]. Buy only if you're handy with a spanner, or if the car has a documented recent engine rebuild.
Explore W245 B-Class →
________________


W246 B-Class (2011–2018)
Years: 2011–2018 • Key Engines: OM651 2.1 CDI (diesel), M270 1.6/2.0 petrol, M274 2.0 turbo petrol — 🔥 Most Enquired — 157 enquiries (2025) [MB‑VERIFIED]
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: This is the B-Class to buy. The OM651 diesel is a known, well-understood engine — the "Black Death" injector seal risk is the main caveat, but it's manageable, preventable, and cheap to fix if caught early [PATTERN DATA]. At £5,000–£14,000 vehicle value against £2,800–£4,800 recon cost, replacement makes solid sense [MB‑QUOTE]. The W246 carries a ✅ Safe verdict for most examples — it's the family MPV that actually works.
Explore W246 B-Class →
________________


Key comparison: The W245 (2005–2011) vs W246 (2011–2018) — the defining line is the OM640-to-OM651 engine switch. The OM640 is the less dependable diesel; the OM651 is a generation ahead in reliability and parts support. If you're buying a B-Class, buy a W246 with the OM651. Skip the W245 unless you're confident with its history. Read the full comparison: W245 vs W246 →

	£2,000–£3,800 [MB-QUOTE]
	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? OM651 rating: 90% confidence — based on 157 [MB‑VERIFIED] enquiries and cross‑referenced with the known OM651 "Black Death" injector‑seal failure pattern that dominates our diesel data across C‑Class and E‑Class [MB‑VERIFIED]. OM640 ratings: Expert‑knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist interviews, not proprietary volume data, because the W245 B‑Class enquiry volume is below the dataset's threshold for reliable annual figures [PATTERN DATA].
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
* Correction applied: The W245 B‑Class's diesel engine is the OM640 (2.0 CDI), not OM646 — OM646 was used in the C‑Class and E‑Class of similar era. The OM640 is a different engine family (single‑cam, EU4 emissions, different injector layout) and has a materially different failure profile (more EGR/injector‑related than timing‑chain). Corrected across all tables.
* Correction applied: The OM651 engine in the W246 B‑Class is the same OM651 fitted to the W205 C‑Class and W212 E‑Class — the "Black Death" injector‑seal failure pattern applies equally. The 157 [MB‑VERIFIED] enquiries for W246 B‑Class are a genuine model‑level count, not a derived figure.
* Verification check: M266, M270 and M274 petrol engine codes confirmed against factory technical specs. The M270's 1.6‑litre (M270 DE16 LA) and 2.0‑litre (M270 DE20 LA) variants are both present; M274 is the turbo petrol used in the B‑Class from 2015 [MB‑SPEC].
* No further corrections needed — source data matched live verification.
________________


SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common B-Class Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The failures B-Class owners actually encounter — ranked by severity and backed by 177+ real UK enquiries.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	OM651 "Black Death" Injector Seal Failure
	The defining diesel issue across all OM651‑powered Mercedes models — copper injector seals degrade, allowing combustion gases to leak past and form a thick carbon "tar" around the injector base. Left untreated, the carbon can weld the injector into the cylinder head, turning a £400–£1,200 seal job into a full cylinder‑head repair or engine replacement. Most common on B‑Class W246 examples from 2012–2016 with 70,000–100,000 miles. [MB‑VERIFIED]
	🔴 Catastrophic if Ignored
	Read more →
	2
	OM640 Injector & EGR Failure
	The earlier W245 diesel — injector leak‑off issues and EGR valve clogging are both common. The OM640's EU4 emissions hardware was more sensitive than the later OM651; repair costs typically £300–£800 per injector, £400–£1,200 for EGR replacement. Pre‑2008 units particularly affected [PATTERN DATA].
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	3
	M270/M274 Turbocharger Oil Starvation
	On the 1.6‑litre and 2.0‑litre turbo petrol variants, the turbocharger's oil supply line can become restricted (sludge buildup from extended service intervals). Warning signs: high‑pitched whine under acceleration, oil consumption above 1L/1,000 miles. Typically occurs 60,000–100,000 miles [PATTERN DATA].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	4
	M266 Petrol Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	The 1.5‑litre and 2.0‑litre naturally‑aspirated petrols in the W245 — timing chain tensioner wear can lead to chain rattle on cold start. Unlike the OM651's rear‑chain complexity, the M266's chain is front‑mounted, making inspection and replacement relatively straightforward. Still, if ignored, the chain can skip and the engine will need a rebuild [PATTERN DATA].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	AdBlue System Issues (W246 facelift, 2014+)
	Post‑2014 W246 B‑Class with OM651 diesel — AdBlue system warnings are the most common non‑engine failure in our W246 B‑Class data. Typically NOx sensor or heater failure, software‑manageable, £400–£1,200 repair. Not a showstopper, but frustrating and needs addressing [MB‑VERIFIED].
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	6
	DPF Clogging (Urban‑Use B‑Class)
	Both OM640 and OM651 diesels — DPF regen cycles fail to complete on short, cold urban trips. The B‑Class is often bought as a city MPV, which directly exacerbates this problem. Symptoms: limp mode, fuel‑diluted oil, £400–£1,200 for DPF clean or replacement. The fix is a 30‑minute motorway run at 2,500rpm once a month — but many owners don't know that [MB‑VERIFIED].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: B-Class Market Intelligence — What 177+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	W246 B‑Class Enquiries
	157 enquiries — the dominant B‑Class generation [MB‑VERIFIED]
	⬆ Rising [PLACEHOLDER — YoY % from source data]
	W245 B‑Class Enquiries
	20 enquiries — lower volume, ageing‑host market [MB‑VERIFIED]
	➡ Stable [PLACEHOLDER — YoY % from source data]
	OM651‑vs‑OM640 Split
	88% of B‑Class enquiries are W246‑generation OM651; just 12% are W245 OM640 [MB‑VERIFIED]
	➡ Stable
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The B‑Class's 177 total enquiries make it one of the quieter Mercedes models in our 2025 dataset — but the 88% / 12% split between W246 and W245 is the sharpest generational divide in the entire Mercedes line‑up. Buyers are actively avoiding the first‑generation W245; the second‑generation W246 accounts for almost nine out of ten enquiries. 
* The OM651's "Black Death" injector‑seal failure is the single biggest driver of B‑Class diesel enquiries — but because it's a cheap, preventable fix if caught early, the OM651 is still the recommended engine. The W245's OM640 doesn't have the same known‑issue‑with‑known‑fix profile; its failures are more varied and less predictably repairable. 
* The B‑Class is the only Mercedes MPV that carries a ✅ Safe verdict without major caveat in our data — the V‑Class and Viano are larger and more expensive to run; the B‑Class is the genuinely family‑friendly option.

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every B-Class Buyer
Buy a W246 B‑Class from 2014 onwards with the OM651 diesel, and ignore the service‑history‑scare stories — the "Black Death" injector seal is a cheap, manageable fix if caught early. The W245 isn't necessarily a bad car, but at current market values, a new engine will cost more than the car is worth. The generation‑based advice on the B‑Class is as clear as it gets.

The B-Class rule — generation is everything: A W245 B200 CDI (OM640) at typical market value of £1,500–£5,000 against a £2,800–£4,800 recon cost sits at 56–96% — at or above our 70% threshold for many examples [THIRD-PARTY]. The honest advice: unless the car has sentimental value or a documented recent engine, walk away from a W245 diesel with a failed engine. A W246 B200 CDI (OM651) at £5,000–£14,000 against the same £2,800–£4,800 recon cost sits at 20–56% — comfortably below the threshold in most cases [MB-QUOTE]. Fix the W246; scrap the W245 (unless it's a low‑mileage, well‑kept petrol example).

SECTION 9 — HOW THE B-CLASS ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the B-Class Engine Range Evolved — 2005 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	First Generation — W245 Launch
	2005–2011
	OM640 2.0 CDI, M266 1.5/2.0 petrol
	Mercedes entered the compact MPV segment with the B-Class — the first car to use the "sandwich floor" chassis (engine and transmission partially under the cabin).
	The OM640 diesel is an older EU4 design — injector and EGR failures are common on higher‑mileage examples. The M266 petrols are simpler and more reliable but less economical.
	Second Generation — W246 Launch
	2011–2014
	OM651 2.1 CDI, M270 1.6/2.0 turbo petrol
	Mercedes updated the B-Class to use the MFA platform (same as A‑Class) — a far more modern architecture that allowed turbocharging across the range and full EU5 compliance.
	This is the defining era. The OM651 is the defining diesel of a generation — 157 enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], and the "Black Death" injector seal failure is the only major caveat.
	W246 Facelift
	2014–2018
	OM651 (updated), M270 (updated), M274 2.0 turbo petrol
	Facelift electronics, emissions hardware (AdBlue on post‑2014 diesels), and a more refined cabin. The M274 replaced the M270 2.0‑litre petrol as a higher‑output option.
	Post‑2014 W246 B‑Class with OM651 is the sweet spot — AdBlue system issues are the main (and low‑severity) caveat. The B‑Class is the ✅ Safe family MPV.
	Third Generation — W247
	2018–present
	OM654 (diesel), M282 1.3 turbo petrol (Renault‑sourced)
	Emissions‑driven: the OM654 replaced the OM651 across the Mercedes range, and the M282 1.3‑litre petrol is Renault‑sourced (a platform‑sharing decision with the A‑Class).
	The W247 B‑Class is outside our core 2025 data — but it's a less‑common UK choice, with the A‑Class taking most of the compact‑Mercedes market.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A B-CLASS? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a B-Class?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	★★★★★
	The B‑Class is the most family‑friendly compact Mercedes in our data — the W246 has the same OM651 engine as the C‑Class but with higher‑roof practicality and lower purchase prices. Buy a 2014–2018 B200 CDI, ignore the "MPV" stigma, and enjoy a safe, reliable family car that doesn't cost a fortune to run.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★★☆
	The OM651 diesel is the motorway‑mile champion — 45–55 mpg, comfortable ride, and enough power for A‑road overtakes. The B‑Class is less common than the A‑Class as a commuter car, but that means lower prices and less competition in the used market.
	Budget Owner
	⚠️ Watch
	This is where the generation split matters. A W245 B‑Class (2005–2011) can be bought for £1,500–£3,000, but a £2,800–£4,800 recon engine is an existential threat to the car's value — you're one failure away from scrapping it. A W246 (2011–2018) at £5,000–£8,000 is a safer budget bet — recon cost sits at 35–60% of vehicle value, which is manageable [MB‑QUOTE].
	Practical Buyer
	★★★★★
	If you need a Mercedes badge but don't want the running costs of an E‑Class or the complexity of a GLC, the B‑Class is the sensible choice. It's the only Mercedes MPV with a ✅ Safe verdict across most examples — the V‑Class is expensive to run and the Viano is discontinued. The B‑Class is the budget‑friendly practical Mercedes.
	First-Time Premium Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The B‑Class is often someone's first Mercedes — the W246 offers the brand's quality, safety and diesel‑reliability at a price that doesn't intimidate. Buy the 2014+ OM651, budget £400–£800 for a preventative injector‑seal check, and you'll have a car that's genuinely cheaper to run than many non‑premium alternatives [EM‑INTERVIEW].
	________________


SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My B-Class Engine — or Should I Replace It?
The B‑Class's economics are sharply divided by generation — a W246 is almost always worth fixing; a W245 diesel often isn't. If your W246 B‑Class has an injector‑seal failure (the most common B‑Class diesel problem in our data), a £400–£1,200 repair is a no‑brainer [MB‑VERIFIED]. But if the engine has suffered terminal bottom‑end damage (rare on the OM651), the repair‑vs‑replace decision is about to get harder — use our calculator to get a personalised verdict.

SECTION 13 — B-CLASS FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: B-Class Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does a B-Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
A B‑Class engine replacement ranges from £2,800–£4,800 for a reconditioned unit (supply‑only) [MB‑QUOTE]. Labour adds 7–10 hours at £65–£95/hr independent, or £110–£160/hr at a London/SE dealer — so fitted cost is roughly £3,200–£5,800. Used engines start at £500–£1,200. The cheapest option is the W245 M266 petrol (£400–£900 used); the most expensive is the OM651 diesel recon (£2,800–£4,800). Use our calculator for a personalised quote based on your specific engine, generation and location → [/fault-diagnostic-calculator?model=b-class]
Q2: Which B-Class engine is the most reliable?
The OM651 2.1 CDI diesel (W246 B‑Class, 2011–2018) is the most reliable B‑Class engine in our data — it accounts for 157 of 177 total B‑Class enquiries, and the "Black Death" injector‑seal failure is a cheap, preventable fix if caught early [MB‑VERIFIED]. The M270 1.6/2.0 turbo petrol is a close second; the OM640 in the W245 is the least dependable.
Q3: Which B-Class engine should I avoid?
The OM640 2.0 CDI in the W245 (2005–2011) carries the highest risk — injector, EGR and DPF failures are all common, and the engine's EU4 emissions hardware is more sensitive than the OM651. More importantly, a £2,800–£4,800 recon cost frequently exceeds the £1,500–£5,000 vehicle value of a W245 B‑Class [MB‑QUOTE]. Avoid the W245 diesel unless it has a documented recent engine rebuild and you're paying sub‑£2,000 for the car.
Q4: Is the B-Class timing chain problem real?
On the OM651 diesel, the timing chain is front‑mounted (unlike the N47 BMW engine's rear‑chain design), so chain wear is less frequent and less catastrophic. The main OM651 caveat is the injector‑seal failure ("Black Death"), not the chain. On the M266 petrol (W245), chain tensioner wear can occur but is rare — and the chain is accessible, making inspection and replacement straightforward. If you hear a rattle on cold start from a W245 M266, have it inspected; it's unlikely to be terminal.
Q5: Is it worth replacing a B-Class engine?
It depends entirely on generation. A W246 B‑Class (2011–2018) is almost always worth fixing — £2,800–£4,800 recon against a £5,000–£14,000 vehicle value sits at 20–56% of value, well below our 70% threshold [MB‑QUOTE]. A W245 B‑Class (2005–2011) with a failed diesel engine is often not worth replacing — the recon cost can reach 56–96% of the car's value. Scrap it and buy a W246 instead. Use our calculator for a personalised assessment.
Q6: Which B-Class should I buy?
Buy a W246 B‑Class from 2014 onwards with the OM651 diesel — the post‑facelift electronics are better, the AdBlue system is software‑manageable (not a mechanical liability), and the OM651 is the most reliable B‑Class engine in our data [EM‑INTERVIEW]. Avoid the W245 diesel; the W245 petrol can be a budget‑friendly option if you're willing to accept 30–35 mpg and higher tax.
Q7: How do I find my B-Class engine code?
The easiest way is to check your VIN plate (visible through the windscreen or on the driver's door pillar) — the engine code is the first three characters of the engine number (e.g. "OM651" for the 2.1‑litre diesel). Alternatively, check your vehicle's registration document (V5C), which lists the engine number. If you're still unsure, use our identification tool — we'll match your VIN to the correct engine and show you the exact replacement cost → [/fault-diagnostic-calculator?model=b-class]
Q8: B-Class vs A-Class — which is more reliable?
The B‑Class (W246) and A‑Class (W176/W177) share the same MFA platform and the same OM651 diesel engine — reliability is identical for the engine itself. The difference is economics: the A‑Class is more common, has a wider market, and tends to hold value better; the B‑Class is a more practical, family‑friendly shape that's often cheaper to buy used. Buy on condition and generation, not badge. The W246 B‑Class's ✅ Safe verdict is one of the strongest in the Mercedes range. Read the full comparison: B‑Class vs A‑Class →

If you hear a rattle on cold start from a W245 M266, have it inspected; it's unlikely to be terminal."
          }

SCHEMA INTEGRITY NOTE (internal — not published)
* AggregateRating omitted — No real, verifiable review data currently exists for mercedesengine.uk across the 2025 data set. Schema block omitted to avoid misrepresenting the site's trust signal.
* LocalBusiness omitted — mercedesengine.uk is an online marketplace connecting owners to vetted specialists, not a physical workshop. Organization schema is the correct type for this business model.
* Car schema omitted — this is a model-level page, not a vehicle listing page. Car schema would be appropriate on a page selling a specific car, but here it would be misleading. The WebPage + Service + Dataset combination correctly describes the page's purpose.
* EngineSpecification schema omitted — this becomes relevant on dedicated Engine Code Pages (e.g., /engines/om651/). On the model-level page, the Engine Database table is correctly represented via the ItemList schema block.
* Data coverage note: The OM640 and M266 entries in the Engine Database are marked [PATTERN DATA] (expert-knowledge tier) as the proprietary 2025 data doesn't reach W245-era enquiry volumes at a reliable level. The OM651's 157 enquiries are the only [MB-VERIFIED] figure — the schema's Dataset block correctly reflects this.

CLA
Step 0 — Model Classification (internal working note, not published copy)
Classification: Which Generation/Engine
Justification: The CLA spans two distinct generations (C117 2013–2019, C118 2019–2025) with a wide spread of engine options and reliability profiles — from the OM651 diesel and M270 petrol in the first generation to the M282 petrol, OM654 diesel and AMG M139 in the second. Buyers need a clear verdict on which generation and which engine to target.

Tag Pill: Mercedes‑Benz • CLA • 2013–present • 2 Generations • M270 • M282 • OM651 • OM654 • M139

Sub-headline: Every CLA engine — M270 petrol, OM651 diesel, M282 petrol, OM654 diesel, and the AMG M139 — across two generations of Mercedes’ four-door coupé. Real UK costs, reliability data and ownership verdicts for the C117 (2013–2019) and C118 (2019–2025). Backed by real UK enquiry data from Mercedes-Benz owners in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 Real UK Enquiries (2025) [MB-VERIFIED] • 🔧 2 Generations Covered • 📖 10+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders

Data-integrity note (internal, not published): The CLA does not have its own model‑level enquiry figure in EM_Proprietary_Data. This page draws on per‑engine enquiry data for engines shared with other Mercedes models (OM651, OM654, M276 etc.), tagged [MB-VERIFIED] where the engine appears in the proprietary file. All model‑level request counts are therefore derived from engine‑level data, not from a standalone CLA‑specific figure. This is noted transparently in the Market Intelligence section.

★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The CLA that started it all — four‑door coupé styling on the A‑Class platform. The M270 petrols are robust; the OM651 diesel carries the “Black Death” injector‑seal caveat and rear‑chain complexity. Buy on condition and documented history, not mileage.

★★★★☆
Our Verdict: Longer, wider, and based on the W177 A‑Class platform — the C118 is the CLA to buy. The M282 petrol is reliable and cheap to run; the OM654 diesel is the cleanest modern Mercedes oil‑burner; the M139 AMG is a fire‑breathing specialist’s toy. Post‑facelift (2023+) electronics are the sweet spot.
