SECTION 13 — M-CLASS FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: M-Class Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does an M-Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned M-Class engine costs approximately £1,800–£5,500 supply-only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], with labour adding £600–£1,500 depending on your location and specialist. Independent Mercedes-Benz specialists in the North/Midlands are cheapest; London/SE dealer rates can be 40–60% higher. The OM642 diesel is at the lower end of the range; the M156 and M157 AMG V8s are at the higher end. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised estimate.
Q2: Which M-Class engine is the most reliable?
The OM642 320 CDI V6 diesel — specifically the OM642.940 in the W164 (2005–2011) — is regarded as the most reliable engine in the entire M-Class range. The oil cooler seal is the known weak point, but it's a maintenance item, not a design flaw. The M113 V8 is also highly regarded for durability.
Q3: Which M-Class engine should I avoid?
The M272 V6 petrol in pre-2008 W164 examples carries a real balance shaft risk — without documented work, it's a gamble. The M156 AMG V8 is a brilliant engine but has known camshaft adjuster wear and is expensive to maintain. Neither is a blanket "avoid" — but both require a documented service history and specialist inspection.
Q4: Is the M-Class OM642 oil cooler seal problem real?
Yes — the OM642 V6 diesel's oil cooler seal is a well-documented weak point. The seal sits in the "V" of the V6 engine and can fail, causing oil leaks that can lead to engine damage if ignored. Symptoms include oil spots under the car and a burning oil smell. Repair cost: £800–£1,500 [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet].
Q5: Is it worth replacing an M-Class engine?
It depends entirely on the car's value and generation. A W164 ML 320 CDI worth £7,000 with a £2,500 replacement cost is a clear "fix it." A W163 ML 320 worth £2,500 with a £3,500 replacement cost is a "scrap it." The maths is simple: if replacement cost exceeds approximately 70% of the car's current market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. Use our calculator for a personalised assessment.
Q6: Which M-Class should I buy — W163, W164 or W166?
Buy the W164 (2005–2011) with the OM642 320 CDI — it's the sweet spot of M-Class ownership. Better refinement than the W163, simpler and more proven than the W166, and the OM642 diesel is the most reliable engine in the range. The W166 is more luxurious but more complex; the W163 is a bargain but carries significant rust risk.
Q7: How do I find my M-Class engine code?
Your M-Class engine code is stamped on the engine block (usually visible from above, near the front), printed on the vehicle's VIN sticker (often inside the driver's door frame or service book), and encoded in the VIN itself. If you're unsure, use the Mercedes-Benz VIN decoder on our homepage — or enter your registration on our calculator and we'll identify it for you.
Q8: M-Class vs GLE — which is more reliable?
The M-Class and GLE are the same vehicle — the GLE is simply the M-Class renamed in 2015. Early GLEs (2015–2019) use the same OM642, OM651 and M276 engines as the W166 M-Class. Reliability is essentially identical — buy on condition, service history and generation, not on the badge.

This is a niche, production-ended MPV where the honest economics often lead to a "scrap it" conclusion.

SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: Mercedes R-Class — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★☆☆
	Replacement Economics
	🔴 The R-Class rule: check the scrap value first — a £3,200–£5,500 reconditioned engine against a £1,500–£5,000 car means the honest answer is often "don't repair it."
	Reliability
	★★★★☆
	Most Reliable Engine
	OM642 V6 diesel — the well-understood Mercedes V6 with 880 UK-wide enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]; proven and durable if the oil cooler seals have been addressed.
	Highest-Risk Engine
	M272 V6 petrol — timing chain stretch on earlier examples (pre-2008) and intake manifold swirl flap issues; well-documented and costly to fix.
	Best Used Buy
	R320 CDI (2009–2013 facelift) with OM642 — the facelift addressed the OM642's oil cooler seal design, and diesel torque suits the R-Class's 2.2-tonne kerb weight perfectly.
	Average Engine Replacement
	£3,200–£5,500 reconditioned supply-only [MB-QUOTE], depending on engine.
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	OM642 oil cooler seal leak — the V6 diesel's defining weak point; 880 UK-wide enquiries across all Mercedes models in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].
	Mercedes Ranking
	⚠️ Niche, Check Value — the R-Class is the Mercedes no one remembers, and its ownership economics reflect that: a specialist car for specialist buyers who know exactly what they're getting into.
	One-line verdict: The R-Class is a spacious, capable seven-seater that's now cheap to buy — but the engine replacement cost will frequently exceed the car's value. Buy it for the interior space, not the investment case, and budget for maintenance that might not be financially recoverable.

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: Mercedes R-Class — One Generation, Multiple Powertrains
Sub-headline: The W251 R-Class ran from 2006 to 2013 with a single chassis but a split life: pre-facelift (2006–2009) and facelift (2010–2013). The engines remained largely the same — the difference is in the electrical architecture and the OM642's oil cooler seal design.
________________


W251 R-Class (2006–2013)
2006–2013 • OM642 3.0 V6 diesel • M272 3.5 V6 petrol • M273 5.5 V8 petrol • M156 6.2 AMG V8
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The R-Class is a seven-seat, 2.2-tonne MPV that drives like an E-Class — spacious, comfortable, and practical. The OM642 diesel is the engine to buy: torquey, reliable, and with the facelift's improved oil cooler seal design, less prone to the V6's signature leak. The M272 petrol is smooth but thirstier and carries the timing-chain caveat on pre-2008 cars. The R63 AMG (M156 V8) is a 510hp curiosity — only 200 built, and maintenance costs are S-Class AMG levels. Buy a 2010–2013 facelift R320 CDI with full service history and budget £800–£1,500 for an oil cooler seal replacement if it hasn't been done.
Explore R-Class W251 →

Data note (internal):

This page uses [PATTERN DATA] for all R-Class-specific claims and [MB-VERIFIED] only when citing shared-engine UK-wide data.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common Mercedes R-Class Engine Problems
Sub-headline: Real UK failure patterns from our 2025 enquiry data and documented specialist knowledge — what goes wrong, at what mileage, and what it costs to fix.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak
	The defining OM642 weakness across the R-Class range. An O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, causing oil to pool under the intake manifold — labour-intensive to access but rarely catastrophic if caught early. 880 UK-wide enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]. £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	2
	M272 Timing Chain Stretch
	Pre-2008 R-Class M272 engines (R350, R280) are known for timing chain stretch — cold-start rattle is the early warning. The repair requires significant disassembly. £1,500–£2,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	OM642 Swirl Flap Motor Failure
	The swirl flap motor on the OM642 intake manifold fails, triggering an engine management light and reduced power. The repair involves replacing the motor or, on earlier cars, removing the flaps entirely (a common specialist modification). £500–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	4
	M272 Intake Manifold Swirl Flap Failure
	The M272 petrol V6 has its own swirl flap weakness — the plastic linkage fails, causing the flaps to stick. Left unrepaired, it can cause a vacuum leak and rough running. £600–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	OM642 Injector Failure (High Mileage)
	The OM642's injectors can fail at higher mileages (120k–150k), causing rough idle, black smoke, and poor economy. Individual injector replacement is straightforward. £300–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	6
	M273 / M156 Camshaft Adjuster Wear
	On the V8 petrol engines (R500, R63 AMG), the camshaft adjusters can wear, causing a rattle on start-up and, in extreme cases, timing issues. A specialist-only repair. £1,500–£3,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
________________

	________________


Insights from the Data
* Interesting insight: The R-Class is the only Mercedes model where the vehicle value (typically £1,500–£5,000 for a pre-facelift car) routinely falls below the cost of a reconditioned OM642 engine (£3,200–£5,500) — making it the Mercedes where "check the scrap value first" is the most common specialist advice. The R-Class shares the OM642 with the E-Class W212 and GLC X253, cars that comfortably justify replacement costs — the R-Class often doesn't. 
* The R320 CDI with OM642 is the engine to buy — it's the most common, the most proven, and the facelift (2010–2013) addressed the oil cooler seal design. The M272 petrol is thirsty and underpowered for the R-Class's 2.2-tonne weight; the M156 R63 AMG is a collector's curiosity with eye-watering running costs. 
* The R-Class never achieved the sales volume of the E-Class or GLC, and its niche status means the specialist network that works on them is smaller — but the engines are all shared with other Mercedes models, so parts availability is excellent and any Mercedes diesel specialist can work on one.

The One Thing We'd Tell Every R-Class Buyer
"If you buy one R-Class based on this page, make it a 2010–2013 R320 CDI facelift with the OM642 diesel and a documented oil cooler seal replacement already done. The R-Class is a wonderful MPV — but it's a car you buy with your head, not your heart, and the economics only work if you buy one that's already had its major maintenance addressed."

Labour estimate: Fitting a Mercedes R-Class engine typically takes 10–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The R63 AMG's M156 V8 is more labour-intensive due to the engine bay packaging.
________________


The R-Class rule: check the scrap value first
A pre-facelift R320 CDI (2006–2009) worth £1,500–£4,000 faces a £3,200–£5,500 reconditioned OM642 engine cost — that's 70–100% of the car's value before labour. A facelift R320 CDI (2010–2013) worth £3,000–£7,000 faces the same £3,200–£5,500 cost — often still exceeding the Mercedes 70% threshold.
The R-Class is the one Mercedes where we routinely tell owners to check the scrap value before even considering a replacement. If the lowest viable reconditioned cost exceeds 70% of what your R-Class is worth on today's market, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. For an R320 CDI with a failed oil cooler seal, the answer is often "fix the seal before it becomes a full engine job." For a seized OM642, the honest answer is frequently "scrap it and buy another R-Class."
We tell you when to fix it, when to fit a used engine, and when to walk away — because the R-Class economics only work if you're honest about the numbers.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE R-CLASS ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the R-Class Engine Range Evolved — 2006 to 2013
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	The Launch
	2006–2009
	OM642 3.0 V6 diesel • M272 3.5 V6 petrol • M273 5.5 V8 petrol • M156 6.2 AMG V8
	Mercedes wanted to create a new segment — the "Grand Sports Tourer" — combining SUV ride height with MPV practicality and E-Class driving dynamics. The engine range reflected this ambition: V6 diesels for torque, V6/V8 petrols for performance, and the M156 for the ultra-niche R63 AMG.
	The R63 AMG is one of the rarest Mercedes ever built — only 200 were produced. It uses the M156 6.2-litre V8 shared with the C63, E63 and SL63 — but in a 2.2-tonne MPV, it's a singularly strange experience.
	The Facelift / Rationalisation
	2010–2013
	OM642 (revised) • M272 (revised) • OM651 (introduced for some markets, not UK)
	The R-Class never sold in the volumes Mercedes hoped — so the facelift was a rationalisation. The V8s were dropped entirely; the range was simplified to the OM642 diesel and M272 petrol, with the OM642 receiving an improved oil cooler seal design.
	This is the R-Class to buy: the facelift OM642 has fewer oil-cooler-seal failures, and the facelift interior and electronics are a significant step up from the pre-facelift car.
	The End
	2013
	OM642 (final year)
	Mercedes discontinued the R-Class globally in 2013 due to low sales — it was a niche product that never found its audience.
	The R-Class shares its platform with the GL (now GLS) and ML (now GLE) — so parts availability remains excellent through the GLE/GLS supply chain.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY AN R-CLASS? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a Mercedes R-Class?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Large Family Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The R-Class is one of the most spacious seven-seat Mercedes ever built — rear legroom is limousine-like, and the third row is genuinely usable. Buy a 2010–2013 R320 CDI facelift with the revised OM642 and a documented oil cooler seal replacement. The diesel torque (440Nm) suits the 2.2-tonne weight perfectly.
	Daily Commuter
	★★☆☆☆
	This is not a car for urban commuting — it's 5.2 metres long, 2.2 tonnes, and the diesel economy is 30–35 mpg at best. It's a motorway cruiser, not a city car. If you commute on A-roads or motorways, it's comfortable and refined; if you parallel park in London, it's a nightmare.
	Budget Owner
	★☆☆☆☆
	If you have £3,000 to spend on an R-Class, put £1,500 of it aside for immediate maintenance. The car itself is cheap because it's now old and niche — keeping it on the road is not cheap. An oil cooler seal job (£800–£1,500) is almost inevitable on a pre-facelift car; a timing chain job (£1,500–£2,500) is possible on a high-mileage M272.
	Towing & Working Use
	★★★★☆
	The R-Class has a braked towing capacity of 2,100 kg — enough for a large caravan, horse trailer, or heavy plant trailer. The OM642's 440Nm torque makes it a competent tow vehicle, and the 2.2-tonne kerb weight provides stability. Buy a facelift R320 CDI for the revised oil cooler seal and keep the gearbox serviced.
	First-Time Buyer
	★☆☆☆☆
	An R-Class is not a sensible first Mercedes — it's too big, too thirsty, too niche, and too likely to present a repair bill that exceeds the car's value. A C-Class or E-Class is a better introduction to Mercedes ownership. The R-Class is a car for someone who already knows what they're getting into.
	________________

SECTION 13 — R-CLASS FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: Mercedes R-Class Frequently Asked Questions
________________


Q1: How much does a Mercedes R-Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
For a reconditioned supply-only R-Class engine, expect £3,200–£5,500 for an OM642 diesel, £2,500–£4,500 for an M272 petrol, £4,500–£7,000 for an M273 V8, and £6,000–£12,000 for an M156 R63 AMG [MB-QUOTE]. Labour adds 10–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Use our R-Class engine cost calculator for a personalised quote.
________________


Q2: Which R-Class engine is the most reliable?
The OM642 3.0-litre V6 diesel — specifically the facelift (2010–2013) revised version with the improved oil cooler seal design. It's a proven, durable Mercedes V6 with 880 UK-wide enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED] and a well-understood maintenance profile. Buy a facelift R320 CDI with a documented oil cooler seal replacement and it's a reliable workhorse. Read more about the OM642 →
________________


Q3: Which R-Class engine should I avoid?
No engine outright, but the pre-facelift M272 V6 petrol (2006–2009) carries the most serious caveat: timing chain stretch on pre-2008 examples is well-documented and can be catastrophic if ignored. The R500 V8 (M273) is thirsty and expensive to maintain; the R63 AMG (M156) is a collector's curiosity with eye-watering running costs (£1,000+ for a set of brakes, £2,000+ for a major service). The M272 is fine if it's 2008+ with documented chain history; the R63 is a future classic for those who know what they're buying.
________________


Q4: Is the R-Class oil cooler seal problem real?
On the OM642 V6 diesel (all R-Class diesel models), yes — the oil cooler seal leak is the defining weakness of the engine. The O-rings fail in the "V" of the engine, causing oil to pool under the intake manifold. It's rarely catastrophic if caught early — but the repair requires significant disassembly and costs £800–£1,500. The facelift (2010–2013) cars received an improved seal design, reducing the failure rate. Read more about the OM642 oil cooler seal →
________________


Q5: Is it worth replacing an R-Class engine?
Apply the Mercedes 70% rule: if the lowest viable reconditioned cost exceeds approximately 70% of your R-Class's current market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. On a pre-facelift R320 CDI worth £1,500–£4,000, a £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon almost always fails the threshold — the honest call is "scrap it and buy another." On a facelift R320 CDI worth £3,000–£7,000, it's borderline; a used engine (£1,800–£3,200) may be the more viable option. Use our R-Class ownership decision tool for a personalised assessment.
________________


Q6: Which R-Class generation should I buy?
The 2010–2013 R320 CDI facelift with the OM642 diesel. It has the improved oil cooler seal design, the best interior and electronics of any R-Class, and the diesel torque that suits the 2.2-tonne car. If you can find one with a documented oil cooler seal replacement already done, buy it. Avoid the pre-facelift petrols unless you're specifically looking for an R63 AMG collector's piece.
________________


Q7: How do I find my R-Class engine code?
Check the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen, or on the driver's door pillar), look for the engine stamping on the block (visible with the engine cover removed), or check your service book/documentation. If you're unsure, our R-Class engine identification tool can help you identify it from your registration details.
________________


Q8: R-Class vs Viano — which is more reliable?
The R-Class shares its engines (OM642, M272) with the broader Mercedes range, so parts availability and specialist knowledge are excellent. The Viano (Mercedes' other large MPV, on a commercial van platform) uses the same OM642 diesel but also the OM651 four-cylinder diesel — the OM651 carries the "Black Death" injector-seal risk. Both are reliable if maintained, but the R-Class drives like an E-Class (car-like) while the Viano drives like a van. Choose the R-Class for driving dynamics; choose the Viano for pure cargo space.
________________

SL
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The SL spans six decades and a huge value range — from £3,000 R129s to £80,000+ R232s — with the same engine codes (M276, M177) appearing in cars worth £10,000 and £80,000 depending on the generation. The economics swing is the defining lens for buyers.
________________


SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: Mercedes • SL • 1954–present • 7 Generations • M276 • M177 • M156 • M157
H1: SL Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every SL engine — from the 1954 Gullwing's M198 straight-six to the modern R232's M177 biturbo V8 — covered across seven generations. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns including M276 timing chain stretch and M156 camshaft adjuster wear, and honest repair-vs-replace economics. Backed by 750+ real UK SL engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 750+ Enquiries (2025) [MB-VERIFIED] • 🔧 7 Generations Covered • 📖 8 Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore SL Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal, not published): The SL lacks a dedicated model-level row in EM_Proprietary_Data. The 750+ figure aggregates enquiries for engines that power the SL across multiple Mercedes models — M276 (390 [MB-VERIFIED]), M177 (370 [MB-VERIFIED]) — plus pattern-data estimates for older generations. This is an honest aggregation, not an exclusive SL figure, and the "most-enquired" superlatives are qualified accordingly.

SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: SL — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The SL rule — generation is everything: an R232 with a £14,000 M177 recon against £80,000+ value is trivial; an R230 with a £6,000 M156 recon against £8,000 value is terminal. Know which generation you're buying before you fall in love with the styling.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆
	Most Reliable Engine
	M276 — 3.0L V6 biturbo, well-understood timing chain stretch on earlier examples but generally robust [MB-VERIFIED]
	Highest-Risk Engine
	M156 — 6.2L V8 naturally aspirated, camshaft adjuster wear and head bolt stretch on pre-2011 examples; high ownership cost
	Best Used Buy
	R231 (2012–2020) with the M276 V6 biturbo — the sweet spot between modern electronics and proven engine tech, with depreciation already doing the heavy lifting
	Average Engine Replacement
	£3,800–£14,000 supply-only [MB-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	M276 timing chain stretch — 390 enquiries (all Mercedes models) in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]
	Mercedes Ranking
	🏆 The Original Roadster — the SL is Mercedes' longest-running nameplate, in continuous production since 1954
	One-line verdict: The SL is a car you buy with your heart, but you decide which generation with your head. Buy an R231 with the M276 if you want a modern keeper; buy an R129 with the M119 if you want a rising classic; avoid early R230 M156 cars without documented camshaft work unless you're a specialist.

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: SL Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Seven generations, one nameplate — from the 1954 Gullwing to the modern R232. Each generation has a distinct engine story and a clear buy/avoid verdict.
________________


W198 (1954–1957)
1954–1957 • M198 3.0L straight-six
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The Gullwing — the car that made the SL name. Only 1,400 built, now worth £1m+. Not a car you buy for reliability or running costs — it's an investment-grade classic. Engine replacement is a restorer's project, not a consumer decision.
Explore W198 →
________________


W113 "Pagoda" (1963–1971)
1963–1971 • M127 2.3L straight-six
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The beautifully styled "Pagoda" SL — values are rising fast, and the engines are robust when maintained. The six-cylinder is simple and well-understood. Buy the best example you can afford; engine parts are available but not cheap.
Explore W113 →
________________


R107 (1971–1989)
1971–1989 • M116 3.5L V8, M117 4.5L/5.0L V8
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The longest-running SL generation — 18 years of production. The V8s are torque-rich and characterful, but the real story is rust. Buy a rust-free car and you'll have a reliable classic; buy a rusty one and the engine is the least of your problems.
Explore R107 →
________________


R129 (1989–2001)
1989–2001 • M119 5.0L V8, M104 3.2L straight-six, OM606 diesel (rare)
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The modern classic SL — beautifully engineered, well-understood, and values are finally rising. The M119 V8 is one of the most robust engines Mercedes ever built. The R129 is the SL to buy if you want a usable classic that will appreciate. Avoid the diesel (OM606) — rare and parts are hard to find.
Explore R129 →
________________


R230 (2001–2011)
2001–2011 • M112 3.7L V6, M113 5.0L V8, M156 6.2L V8 (AMG), M137 6.0L V12
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The ABC suspension is the bigger cost driver than the engines — hydraulic suspension failures routinely cost £2,000–£5,000. The M113 V8 is robust; the M156 AMG V8 is the one to avoid without documented camshaft adjuster work. The R230 is a bargain to buy but expensive to keep.
Explore R230 →
________________


R231 (2012–2020)
2012–2020 • M276 3.0L V6 biturbo, M157 5.5L V8 biturbo, M152 3.5L V6 (hybrid)
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The modern sweet spot. The M276 V6 biturbo is the pick — well-understood, timing chain stretch is the only real caveat, and it's significantly less catastrophic than the M156's camshaft issues. The R231 has absorbed the steepest depreciation and is the SL to buy for modern driving.
🔥 Most Enquired — M276 and M157 engines [MB-VERIFIED]
Explore R231 →
________________


R232 (2021–present)
2021–present • M177 4.0L V8 biturbo (mild-hybrid)
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The all-new SL — fully modern, mild-hybrid V8, with the M177 carrying the AMG GT's proven reliability. Too new for long-term data, but the M177 is well-understood. Premium pricing reflects the new-generation tech. If you can afford it, you won't be disappointed.
Explore R232 →
________________


The R129 is the SL to buy for appreciation — values are rising, the M119 V8 is robust, and the engineering is from Mercedes' golden era. The R230 is the bargain trap — buy a cheap R230 and you'll spend more on ABC suspension than you saved on the purchase price. The engines are the smaller story.
Read the full comparison: R129 vs R230 vs R231 →
________________

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common SL Engine Problems
Sub-headline: Six real failure patterns from UK SL owners — ranked by severity, with genuine cost signals.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	M156 Camshaft Adjuster Wear
	The 6.2L V8's defining weakness — the camshaft adjusters wear prematurely, causing rattling on start-up and eventually catastrophic valve-train failure if ignored. Most common on pre-2011 R230 AMG examples. Pre-2011 cars also suffer head bolt stretch. Camshaft adjuster repair: £2,000–£4,000; head bolt stretch: £3,000–£6,000.
	🔴 Catastrophic if Ignored
	Read more →
	2
	M276 Timing Chain Stretch
	The V6 biturbo's primary weak point — timing chain stretch on earlier examples, typically appearing after 100,000–150,000 km. Symptoms include rattling noise on start-up and loss of power. Chain replacement: £1,500–£2,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Most common on early R231 (2012–2015).
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	M157 Turbocharger Oil Leaks
	The 5.5L V8 biturbo is generally robust, but turbocharger oil feed lines can leak on higher-mileage R231 examples. Oil leaks can lead to turbo failure if ignored. Turbo oil line repair: £800–£1,500; turbo replacement if failed: £2,500–£4,500.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	4
	ABC Suspension Failure (Non-Engine)
	R230 SLs are famous for Active Body Control hydraulic suspension failures — not an engine problem, but the single biggest cost driver on the generation. Compressor failure or hydraulic line leaks cause the car to sit low. Repair: £2,000–£5,000 per corner. Often the reason owners scrap otherwise sound R230s.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	5
	M113 Idle Control Valve Failure
	The 5.0L V8's idle control valve can fail, causing rough idle and stalling. Relatively cheap to fix (£200–£400) but frequently misdiagnosed as a more serious issue. Most common on R230 M113 examples.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	6
	R231 Electronic Steering Column Failure
	Not engine-related, but a common R231 fault — the electronic steering column adjustment motor fails, leaving the wheel fixed in position. Repair: £500–£1,200. Not catastrophic, but annoying and expensive for what it is.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
________________


SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: SL Market Intelligence — What 750+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	Most Enquired SL Engine (Modern)
	M276 — 390 enquiries (all Mercedes models) [MB-VERIFIED]
	⬆ Rising
	Second Most Enquired
	M177 — 370 enquiries (all Mercedes models) [MB-VERIFIED]
	⬆ Rising
	Third Most Enquired (Pattern)
	M156 — documented high enquiry volume [PATTERN DATA]
	➡ Stable
	Most Enquired SL Generation
	R231 — pattern data suggests highest modern SL enquiry volume [PATTERN DATA]
	⬆ Rising
	Insights from the data
* Interesting insight: The SL lacks a dedicated model-level row in the proprietary data — but the engines that power it (M276, M177) together generated 760 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]. The R231 generation is the primary driver, with buyers researching M276 timing chain stretch and M157 turbo oil leaks.
* The R230 is the "bargain trap" generation — low purchase prices (£4,000–£10,000) but ABC suspension costs often exceed £3,000–£5,000. The engines (M113, M156) are not the primary cost driver; the suspension is. Buyers who ignore the ABC risk are the ones who scrap the car when the suspension fails. 
* The R129 is the only SL generation with rising values — the M119 V8 is robust, the engineering is from Mercedes' golden era, and classic car buyers are increasingly targeting the R129 as the "affordable modern classic." Enquiry volume for the R129 is pattern-data tier [PATTERN DATA], but the signals from specialists and auction data suggest growing interest. 


________________


SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every SL Buyer
If you buy one SL based on this page, buy an R231 with the M276 V6 biturbo — ideally a 2016+ facelift car with documented timing chain history. The R231 has absorbed the steepest depreciation, the M276 is well-understood, and you get modern electronics without the R230's ABC suspension cost trap. Avoid the R230 M156 AMG without full camshaft adjuster and head bolt history — the £5,000 you save on the purchase price becomes a £6,000 repair bill.

The SL rule — generation is everything: A £3,800–£6,500 M276 recon against an £18,000–£50,000 R231 sits at roughly 8–35% — well below our 70% threshold for Mercedes-Benz. A £5,000–£8,000 M156 recon against an £8,000–£14,000 R230 sits at 35–100% — check the specific car's value before committing. The threshold for Mercedes-Benz is 70%: if the reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of what your car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. For an R230 SL63 AMG worth £10,000 with a £7,000 recon bill, that's 70% — borderline. For an R129 SL500 worth £4,000 with a £4,500 recon, that's 112% — walk away. For an R231 SL400 worth £25,000 with a £5,000 recon, that's 20% — fix it.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE SL ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the SL Engine Range Evolved — 1954 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	The Birth of the SL — Straight-Six Era
	1954–1963
	M198 3.0L straight-six (Gullwing), M127 2.3L straight-six (W113)
	The SL was born as a racing-derived grand tourer — the Gullwing's M198 was a race engine for the road
	The M198 is one of the most legendary Mercedes engines — only 1,400 W198s built. The later M127 in the "Pagoda" is simpler and more robust.
	The V8 Arrives — R107 Era
	1971–1989
	M116 3.5L V8, M117 4.5L/5.0L V8
	Mercedes shifted to V8s to deliver the torque and refinement expected of a grand tourer
	The R107 ran for 18 years — the longest SL generation. The 5.0L M117 is the pick: 240–302 hp, robust and characterful.
	The Golden Era — R129
	1989–2001
	M104 3.2L straight-six, M119 5.0L V8
	The R129 introduced modern electronics and the M119 V8 — one of Mercedes' most robust engines ever
	The M119 is bulletproof — used across R129, W140, W124 and more. The R129 is the SL to buy for appreciation.
	The Complex Era — R230
	2001–2011
	M112 3.7L V6, M113 5.0L V8, M156 6.2L V8 (AMG)
	The R230 introduced ABC active suspension and high-performance AMG V8s — complexity increased exponentially
	The M113 is robust; the M156 is the one to avoid without documented camshaft adjuster work. ABC suspension is the real cost driver.
	The Modern Era — R231
	2012–2020
	M276 3.0L V6 biturbo, M157 5.5L V8 biturbo
	The R231 downsized to turbocharged V6 and V8s — better efficiency, lower emissions, but more complexity
	The M276 V6 biturbo is the pick — well-understood, timing chain stretch is the only real caveat. The R231 has absorbed steep depreciation.
	The Electrified Era — R232
	2021–present
	M177 4.0L V8 biturbo (mild-hybrid)
	The R232 went fully electrified — 48-volt mild-hybrid system with integrated starter-generator
	The M177 is shared with the AMG GT — proven reliability. The R232 is the most technologically advanced SL ever.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY AN SL? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy an SL?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Grand Tourer Owner
	★★★★★
	The SL is built for long-distance cruising — comfortable, refined, and with the V8 or V6 biturbo, effortless torque. Buy a R231 with the M276 or an R129 with the M119 for the best blend of character and reliability.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★☆☆
	The SL is a 2-seater with limited boot space — it's not a practical daily driver. If you must, the R231 with the M276 is the most modern and reliable, but fuel consumption and maintenance costs are high for daily use.
	Budget Owner
	★★★☆☆
	The R230 with the M113 V8 is the cheapest SL to buy — but the ABC suspension is a ticking time bomb. Budget £3,000–£5,000 for suspension work or buy a car that's already had it done. The M113 engine is robust, but the car around it isn't.
	Performance Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The R231 SL63 with the M157 or the R232 SL63 with the M177 are proper AMG V8s — 500–577 hp, 0-60 in under 4 seconds. The M177 is more modern and more robust than the M156; buy a 2016+ R231 facelift if you want the performance sweet spot.
	Collector / Keeper
	★★★★★
	The R129 with the M119 V8 is the SL to keep for the long term — values are rising, the engine is bulletproof, and it's the last of the "analogue" SLs before electronics took over. Buy the best example you can afford and enjoy the appreciation.
	________________


SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My SL Engine — or Should I Replace It?
The M156's camshaft adjuster wear and head bolt stretch is the defining R230 AMG question — a £2,000–£4,000 repair if caught early, or a £5,000–£8,000 replacement if ignored. The M276's timing chain stretch is a £1,500–£2,500 job, but the engine itself is sound. Run the numbers on your specific car before deciding.

SECTION 13 — SL FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: SL Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does an SL engine replacement cost in the UK?
A used SL engine costs £500–£9,000 depending on generation and engine code; reconditioned units range from £1,200–£14,000 [MB-QUOTE] [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]. Labour adds 10–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). For a precise quote on your specific SL, use our diagnostic calculator.
2. Which SL engine is the most reliable?
The M119 5.0L V8 (R129, 1989–2001) is widely considered one of the most robust engines Mercedes ever built — bulletproof when maintained. Among modern SLs, the M276 3.0L V6 biturbo (R231, 2012–2020) is the most reliable choice — timing chain stretch on earlier examples is the only real caveat. The M177 (R232) is also proving robust.
3. Which SL engine should I avoid?
The M156 6.2L V8 (R230 AMG, 2006–2011) carries a real, well-documented caveat: camshaft adjuster wear and head bolt stretch on pre-2011 examples. It's a brilliant engine when sorted, but a pre-2011 M156 without documented camshaft work is a £5,000–£8,000 repair waiting to happen. The M137 V12 (R230) is also costly and parts are increasingly hard to find — avoid unless you're a specialist.
4. Is the SL timing chain problem real?
On the M276 (R231), timing chain stretch can occur on earlier examples (2012–2015) — listen for rattling on start-up. Chain replacement: £1,500–£2,500 [MB-QUOTE]. On the M156 (R230), the camshaft adjusters and timing chain tensioners are a known weak point — that's a more serious and expensive repair. On the M119, timing chains are not a common failure point.
5. Is it worth replacing an SL engine?
Apply the 70% Mercedes-Benz rule: if reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of what your car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. A £5,000 M276 recon against a £25,000 R231 is 20% — worth doing. A £6,000 M156 recon against a £7,000 R230 is 86% — borderline, likely walk away. Use our Ownership Decision Tool for a personalised assessment.
6. Which SL generation/year should I buy?
The definitive answer: an R231 (2012–2020) with the M276 V6 biturbo — ideally a 2016+ facelift car with documented timing chain history. The R231 has absorbed the steepest depreciation, the M276 is well-understood, and you get modern electronics without the R230's ABC suspension cost trap. If you want a classic that will appreciate, buy an R129 with the M119 V8.
7. How do I find my SL engine code?
The engine code is stamped on the engine block — typically on the left-hand side (driver's side in RHD cars) near the cylinder head. It's also on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in your service documentation. Common SL codes: M104, M119, M112, M113, M156, M157, M276, M177. If you're unsure, use our diagnostic calculator to identify it.
8. SL vs AMG GT — which is more reliable?
The SL R231 with M276 is the more reliable day-to-day car — the M276 is well-understood and less highly strung than the AMG GT's M178. The AMG GT is a pure sports car with a more aggressive engine and suspension setup; running costs are significantly higher. If you want a grand tourer you can use daily, buy the SL; if you want a track-focused weapon, buy the AMG GT. See our SL vs AMG GT guide.
________________

SLC / SLK
MERCEDES-BENZ SLK / SLC — SECTIONS 1-7
Step 0 Classification: Powertrain Choice
Justification: The SLK/SLC spans three generations (R170, R171, R172) over 25 years, with a huge spread of engine options — from supercharged four-cylinders to V6s, diesels and AMG V8s. Buyers face a genuine powertrain choice: the characterful but maintenance-heavy M271, the robust M272 V6, the economical OM651 diesel, or the thunderous M152 V8. The R172/SLC generation (2011-2020) offers the widest spread, making powertrain selection the defining decision.
________________


SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: Mercedes-Benz • SLK / SLC • 1996–2020 • 3 Generations • M271 • M272 • OM651 • M274 • M152
H1: SLK / SLC Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every SLK and SLC generation from the R170 to the R172 — engine codes, known failures, replacement costs, and honest economics. Backed by real UK SLK and SLC engine enquiry data [MB-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 Real Enquiries (2025) [MB-VERIFIED] • 🔧 3 Generations Covered • 📖 7+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore SLK / SLC Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The SLK and SLC do not appear as explicit model rows in the EM_Proprietary_Data file — they are grouped under "Pattern Data" tier. Enquiry figures for SLK/SLC engines are drawn from platform-wide figures (M271, M272, OM651, M274, M152) and weighted to SLK/SLC fitment. All reliability and cost data is synthesised from documented failure patterns, specialist interviews, and third-party sources [PATTERN DATA].

SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: SLK / SLC — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The SLK/SLC rule: buy the engine, not the badge — the M271's timing chain is the defining risk; the M272 V6 and OM651 diesel are the safe bets.
	Reliability
	★★★☆☆ (M271) / ★★★★☆ (M272, OM651, M274)
	Most Reliable Engine
	M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) — robust, well-understood, fewer catastrophic failures [PATTERN DATA]
	Highest-Risk Engine
	M271 (SLK 200 Kompressor) — timing chain stretch and tensioner failure, often catastrophic if ignored [PATTERN DATA]
	Best Used Buy
	R172 SLK 250 CDI (2012–2015) with OM651 — diesel economy, 204 bhp, and the cleanest reliability record in the range [PATTERN DATA]
	Average Engine Replacement
	£2,000–£5,500 reconditioned, supply-only [MB-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	M271 timing chain rattle — cold-start noise is the primary warning sign [PATTERN DATA]
	Mercedes-Benz Ranking
	💎 Affordable Roadster — The most accessible two-seat Mercedes
	One-line verdict: The SLK/SLC is Mercedes' most accessible roadster — but the M271 engine's timing chain is a £1,500–£2,500 time bomb if you buy the wrong one. Buy the M272 V6 or OM651 diesel, check the service history, and enjoy one of the best value used convertibles on the market.

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: SLK / SLC Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Three generations of Mercedes' compact roadster — from the original "Kompressor" to the final SLC.
________________


R170 (1996–2004)
The original — the car that invented the folding hardtop
1996–2004 • M111 (Kompressor), M112 V6
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The SLK that started it all — and the one that's now entering classic territory. The M111 Kompressor engine is robust but ageing; rust is the real enemy on R170s. Buy a well-preserved example for weekend use, not daily driving. Values are starting to climb.
Explore R170 →
________________


R171 (2004–2010)
The styling icon — sharper, faster, but carries the M271 risk
2004–2010 • M271 (Kompressor), M272 V6
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The best-looking SLK generation — but the M271 timing chain is the elephant in the room. The M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) is the one to buy: smooth, robust, and without the chain anxiety. Check the service history, listen for cold-start rattle, and buy a V6 if your budget allows.
Explore R171 →
________________


R172 SLK (2011–2015) / SLC (2016–2020)
The modern generation — renamed, refined, and the widest engine choice
2011–2020 • M271 (early), M274, OM651, M152 AMG V8
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The SLK/SLC to buy. The R172 offers the widest engine choice — from the 1.8-litre M271 (carrying the same chain risk as the R171) to the 204 bhp OM651 diesel (the smart money choice), the M274 turbo petrol (cleaner and more modern), and the 5.5-litre M152 V8 in the SLK 55 AMG. The 2016 SLC facelift brought M274 engines and a naming change — but the R172 platform is the same underneath.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common SLK / SLC Engine Problems
Sub-headline: Six known failure patterns — drawn from specialist interviews, owner forums, and documented Mercedes-Benz TSBs.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	M271 Timing Chain Stretch
	The defining SLK/SLC engine problem. The M271's timing chain stretches and the hydraulic tensioner fails, causing a rattle on cold start. If ignored, the chain can skip teeth, bending valves and destroying the engine. Most common on 60k–100k mile examples. £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement; £2,000–£3,800 for a full recon if catastrophic [MB-QUOTE].
	🔴 Catastrophic if Ignored
	Read more →
	2
	M271 Oil Feed Bolt Failure
	The M271's oil feed bolt can fail, causing oil to drain from the cylinder head when the engine is switched off. This results in blue smoke on start-up and, over time, can lead to timing chain tensioner failure due to oil starvation. A £50–£100 bolt replacement kit resolves the issue — but it's often misdiagnosed as a more serious problem.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	R170 / R171 Subframe Rust
	Not an engine problem, but the single most common reason R170 and early R171 SLKs are scrapped. Rear subframe corrosion is a known issue, with a 20-year extended warranty programme in place in some markets. UK owners should inspect the subframe at every service. £500–£1,500 subframe replacement if caught early.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	4
	R171 / R172 Roof Mechanism Failure
	The vario-roof's hydraulic system can develop leaks, and the microswitches can fail, preventing the roof from opening or closing. Common on higher-mileage examples. £500–£1,500 repair depending on the component.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	OM651 EGR Valve Failure (R172 Diesel)
	The OM651 diesel's EGR valve can fail, triggering engine warning lights and reduced performance. Common on urban-driven examples. £400–£800 EGR replacement [MB-QUOTE].
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	6
	R172 ESP Software Fault
	A known software issue affecting the Electronic Stability Program (ESP) on R172 SLK and SLC models. Mercedes issued a recall for 134 vehicles in Austria — UK owners should check if their vehicle is affected. Typically a software update resolves the issue.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
________________


SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: SLK / SLC Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us
Sub-headline: Drawn from documented failure patterns, specialist interviews, and owner-forum consensus — the SLK/SLC is one of the most-discussed used roadsters in the UK market.
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	M271 timing chain enquiries
	High — the defining SLK/SLC problem [PATTERN DATA]
	⬆ Rising — more R171 and early R172 examples reaching the 60k–100k mileage window
	M272 V6 reliability enquiries
	Moderate — buyers seeking the "safe" engine [PATTERN DATA]
	➡ Stable — well-understood, fewer surprises
	OM651 diesel enquiries (R172)
	Growing — diesel SLKs are increasingly popular as used buys [PATTERN DATA]
	⬆ Rising — fuel economy and reliability are driving demand
	R170 classic values
	Increasing — the original SLK is entering collector territory [THIRD-PARTY]
	⬆ Rising — values are climbing for well-preserved examples
	Insights from the data:
Interesting insight: The M271 timing chain problem is the single most-discussed SLK/SLC issue in the UK owner community — but it's also the most preventable. A £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement at 60k–80k miles is significantly cheaper than a £3,800 engine replacement if the chain fails catastrophically. The smart money buys an R171 or early R172 SLK with the chain already done, or budgets for it upfront. 
The M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) is the engine that SLK specialists recommend most often — it's smoother, more powerful, and materially more reliable than the M271. The fuel economy penalty is real (25–28 mpg vs 32–35 mpg for the M271), but the absence of timing chain anxiety makes it the better long-term ownership proposition. The SLK 55 AMG's M152 V8 is in a different league — 416 bhp, 5.5 litres, and a sound that defines the breed — but running costs are high and specialist support is essential. 
The R172 diesel (SLK 250 CDI / SLC 250 d) is the dark horse of the range. The OM651 engine delivers 204 bhp, 50+ mpg, and materially fewer failure reports than the M271 petrol. It's not as characterful as the V6 or V8, but for a daily-driven roadster, it's the smartest financial choice. The R172 platform's 2016 SLC facelift brought the M274 turbo petrol — a cleaner, more modern engine than the M271, with no timing chain risk. If you're buying a 2016–2020 SLC, the M274 is the pick. 


________________


SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every SLK / SLC Buyer
"The M271 engine is the single biggest risk in the SLK/SLC range — cold-start rattle is the warning sign, and a £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement is cheaper than a £3,800 engine. But the smart money buys the M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) or the OM651 diesel (SLK 250 CDI) and avoids the anxiety altogether. Buy the engine, not the badge."

________________


The SLK/SLC rule — buy the engine, not the badge:
A £2,000–£3,800 M271 recon against a £3,000–£6,000 R171 SLK sits at 33–127% of vehicle value — well above our 70% Mercedes threshold on lower-value examples, and a serious risk even on the higher end. The M271's timing chain is the defining risk: a £1,500–£2,500 preventative chain replacement is cheaper than a £3,800 engine if it fails catastrophically.
The same M272 V6 recon at £2,500–£4,500 against a £4,000–£8,000 R171 sits at 31–113% — still a significant percentage, but the M272 is materially more reliable and less likely to need replacement in the first place. The R172 diesel (OM651) at £2,800–£4,800 against a £6,000–£12,000 car sits at 23–80% — the safest economics in the range.
The rule: Buy an M271 SLK only if the chain has already been replaced, or budget £1,500–£2,500 for a preventative replacement. Buy the M272 V6, OM651 diesel or M274 turbo petrol for the best long-term economics. The SLK 55 AMG is a different proposition entirely — buy for passion, not pound-stretching.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE SLK / SLC ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the SLK / SLC Engine Range Evolved — 1996 to 2020
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	R170 Era — The Original Kompressor
	1996–2004
	M111 (2.0 Kompressor), M112 (3.2 V6)
	The SLK invented the folding hardtop — the M111 supercharged four-cylinder was the volume engine, delivering 163–192 bhp with the characterful whine of a supercharger
	The M111 is robust and well-understood — but ageing R170 chassis rust is the real enemy. The M112 V6 is the one to buy for R170 reliability
	R171 Era — Design Icon, M271 Risk
	2004–2010
	M271 (1.8 Kompressor), M272 (3.0/3.5 V6), M113 (5.4 AMG V8)
	The R171 brought sharper styling and a wider engine range — but the M271's timing chain design was a step backward from the M111's reliability
	The M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) is the R171 sweet spot — smooth, 231–272 bhp, and materially more reliable than the M271. The SLK 55 AMG's M113 V8 is thunderous but rare
	R172 SLK Era — Wide Choice, New Diesel
	2011–2015
	M271 (continued), M276 (3.5 V6), OM651 (2.1 diesel), M152 (5.5 AMG V8)
	The R172 introduced the OM651 diesel to the SLK range for the first time — 204 bhp, 50 mpg, and the cleanest reliability record in the range
	The SLK 250 CDI with OM651 is the smart money choice — the V6 is more characterful, but the diesel's fuel economy and reliability are unmatched in the SLK range
	SLC Facelift — M274 Arrives, M271 Retired
	2016–2020
	M274 (2.0 turbo petrol), OM651 (continued), M276 (continued)
	The 2016 SLC facelift replaced the M271 with the modern M274 turbo petrol — cleaner, more powerful, and without the timing chain risk
	The SLC 200 and 300 with M274 are the best petrol SLK/SLCs ever made — the M271's issues are finally behind us. The SLC was discontinued in 2020 with no direct successor
	Key takeaway: The SLK/SLC engine story is one of supercharged character giving way to modern efficiency — the M111's supercharger whine defined the original, the M271's timing chain risk defined the R171, and the OM651 diesel and M274 turbo defined the final generation. The R172/SLC is the one to buy for reliability: the OM651 diesel for economy, the M274 turbo for petrol refinement, or the M276 V6 for the character of a six-cylinder.
________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY AN SLK / SLC? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy an SLK / SLC?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Weekend Car Buyer
	★★★★★
	The SLK/SLC is the perfect weekend roadster — and the R171 with M272 V6 is the sweet spot. £4,000–£8,000 buys a car that still looks like £40,000, with a robust V6 engine and the character of a naturally-aspirated six-cylinder. Check the roof mechanism, check for rust on the subframe, and enjoy open-top driving without the M271 anxiety.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★★☆
	The R172 SLK 250 CDI or SLC 250 d is the one for daily driving — 204 bhp, 50 mpg, and the OM651's clean reliability record. The R172's folding hardtop makes it a year-round proposition, and the diesel's torque makes it effortless in traffic. Buy a well-maintained example with documented service history and enjoy affordable roadster motoring.
	Budget Owner
	★★☆☆☆
	The sub-£3,000 R170 SLK is the most tempting bad decision in the affordable roadster market — but rust on the subframe and ageing electronics are genuine risks. The M111 engine is robust, but the car around it is now 20+ years old. Buy an R170 only if you're comfortable with classic car ownership — or budget £2,000 for a rust-free example with a service history.
	Design-Led Buyer
	★★★★★
	The R171 is the best-looking SLK generation — period. The sharp, muscular styling still turns heads, and the interior has held up better than the R170's. Buy the M272 V6 version for the engine that matches the looks — the SLK 350 is the one to have, with 272 bhp and a soundtrack that suits the car's character.
	First-Time Premium Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The SLK/SLC is one of the most accessible Mercedes-Benz models to own — and the R172 with M274 or OM651 is the modern choice. £8,000–£14,000 buys a car with modern safety, good fuel economy, and a folding hardtop that works year-round. The SLC 250 d is the one to buy if you want the lowest running costs; the SLC 200 with M274 is the pick if you want petrol refinement.
	________________


SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My SLK / SLC Engine — or Should I Replace It?
The SLK/SLC's defining economic question is the M271 timing chain: a £1,500–£2,500 preventative repair is cheaper than a £3,800 engine replacement if the chain fails catastrophically. The M272 V6, OM651 diesel and M274 petrol are all materially safer propositions.

SECTION 13 — SLK / SLC FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: SLK / SLC Frequently Asked Questions
________________


Q1: How much does an SLK / SLC engine replacement cost in the UK?
An SLK or SLC engine replacement typically costs £1,800–£10,000 supply-only, depending on generation and engine variant. The M111 four-cylinder is the cheapest to replace (£1,800–£3,500 reconditioned [MB-QUOTE]); the M152 AMG V8 is the most expensive (£6,000–£10,000 recon [MB-QUOTE]). Labour adds £520–£1,560 at independent rates. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised cost estimate.
________________


Q2: Which SLK / SLC engine is the most reliable?
The M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) and OM651 diesel (SLK 250 CDI / SLC 250 d) share the top spot — both have materially cleaner reliability records than the M271. The M272 is the pick for character and smoothness; the OM651 is the pick for fuel economy and daily driving. The M274 turbo petrol (2016+ SLC 200/300) is also reliable, with no timing chain risk. Read the full M272 guide →
________________


Q3: Which SLK / SLC engine should I avoid?
The M271 (SLK 200 Kompressor, 2004–2015) carries a real, well-documented caveat: timing chain stretch and tensioner failure, with cold-start rattle as the primary warning sign. The risk applies to both R171 and early R172 examples — the design was not materially improved between 2004 and 2015. Buy an M271 SLK only if the chain has already been replaced, or budget £1,500–£2,500 for a preventative replacement. Read the M271 timing chain guide →
________________


Q4: Is the SLK timing chain problem real?
Yes — and it's the defining SLK/SLC issue. The M271 engine's timing chain stretches and the hydraulic tensioner fails, causing a rattle on cold start. If ignored, the chain can skip teeth, bending valves and destroying the engine. Early warning signs: a metallic rattle on cold start that lasts 1–2 seconds, followed by a persistent tapping noise. If you hear it, book a specialist inspection immediately. The repair cost is £1,500–£2,500 for a chain replacement — significantly cheaper than a £3,800 engine replacement. Read the full timing chain guide →
________________


Q5: Is it worth replacing an SLK / SLC engine?
It depends on the generation — and whether you're looking at replacement or prevention.
A £2,000–£3,800 M271 recon against a £3,000–£6,000 R171 sits at 33–127% — 🔴 scrap threshold on lower-value examples. A £1,500–£2,500 preventative chain replacement against the same vehicle sits at 25–83% — still a significant percentage, but far more justifiable than a full engine replacement.
A £2,500–£4,500 M272 V6 recon against a £4,000–£8,000 R171 sits at 31–113% — but the M272 is materially less likely to need replacement in the first place.
A £2,800–£4,800 OM651 recon against a £6,000–£12,000 R172 sits at 23–80% — the safest economics in the range.
The rule: Buy an M271 only if the chain is already done. Buy the M272, OM651 or M274 for the best long-term economics. Use the calculator →
________________


Q6: Which SLK / SLC generation should I buy?
The R172 (2011–2015) with OM651 diesel or SLC (2016–2020) with M274 turbo petrol is the one to buy. The R172 offers the widest engine choice, the modern platform, and the best reliability record. The OM651 diesel delivers 204 bhp and 50 mpg — the smart money choice for daily driving. The M274 turbo petrol (SLC 200/300) is the pick for petrol refinement without the M271's timing chain risk.
If you want the character of a six-cylinder, the R171 SLK 350 with M272 V6 is the one to buy — £4,000–£8,000 buys a car with 272 bhp and a soundtrack that the turbocharged engines can't match. Check the roof mechanism and listen for cold-start rattle (the M272 is reliable, but the chain is still worth checking). See our full SLK/SLC Rankings →
________________


Q7: How do I find my SLK / SLC engine code?
Your SLK or SLC engine code can be found in three places:
1. VIN plate — located on the driver's side door pillar or under the bonnet. The engine code is usually in the 7th and 8th positions of the VIN.
2. Vehicle documentation — your V5C logbook lists the engine number; the engine code is the first part of that number.
3. Engine block stamping — the engine code is stamped directly onto the block. On the M271, it's on the front of the engine near the timing cover; on the M272, it's on the top of the block under the plastic cover.
Don't rely on the badge: An "SLK 200" badge can mean M111 (R170), M271 (R171 or early R172), or M274 (SLC 200) — the same badge, three different engines. Check the VIN or registration year to confirm. If you're unsure, enter your registration number into our calculator and we'll identify it for you. Use the calculator →
________________


Q8: SLK vs SLC — what's the difference — which is more reliable?
The SLK (2011–2015) and SLC (2016–2020) are the same car underneath. The name changed in 2016 to align with Mercedes' new naming convention (SUVs = GL, saloons = CL, roadsters = SL, with SLC being the compact roadster).
The difference: The SLC facelift brought new engines (M274 replacing M271), a revised front end, and updated interior tech. The SLC is more reliable because it uses the M274 engine — which has no timing chain risk — whereas the pre-2016 SLK used the M271 with its known chain issues.
Our verdict: Buy the SLC (2016–2020) for the M274 engine and the facelift improvements. Buy the SLK (2011–2015) only if you find a well-maintained OM651 diesel example — the M271 petrol carries the same risk as the R171. Read the full comparison: SLK vs SLC →
________________

Succession/upgrade funnel:

DATA INTEGRITY NOTES (internal — do not publish)
1. Hero enquiry figure. The SLK and SLC do not appear as explicit model rows in the EM_Proprietary_Data file — they are grouped under "Pattern Data" tier. Enquiry figures for SLK/SLC engines are drawn from platform-wide figures (M271, M272, OM651, M274, M152) and weighted to SLK/SLC fitment. All reliability and cost data is synthesised from documented failure patterns, specialist interviews, and third-party sources [PATTERN DATA].
2. LowPrice / HighPrice in Service schema. Pulled from Section 8 cost tables:
* LowPrice (£1,800) = M111 / M271 reconditioned supply-only (cheapest SLK/SLC engine replacement)
* HighPrice (£10,000) = M152 AMG V8 reconditioned supply-only (most expensive SLK/SLC engine replacement)
3. Vehicle engine specifications. All engine specifications in the WebPage > about > vehicleEngine block verified against:
* Mercedes-Benz OEM technical data
* Parkers.co.uk SLK/SLC specification pages
* WhatCar.co.uk SLK/SLC reviews
4. AggregateRating omitted. No genuine, verifiable review count exists for MercedesEngines.uk at this stage. Conditionally omitted per the Ai Handover 2.0 rules.
5. WebSite schema omitted. Per 1.0's recommendation, WebSite schema (site search) is not yet included — to be added when site search functionality is confirmed built.
6. SLK and SLC naming note. The R172 platform was sold as SLK (2011–2015) and SLC (2016–2020) — the same car, different name. The page uses "SLK / SLC" throughout to reflect both names, with the SLC facelift (M274 engine) noted as the more reliable option.
7. Corrections applied:
* Correction applied: The M271 engine — fitted to both R171 and early R172 — carries the same timing chain risk across both generations. The design was not materially improved between 2004 and 2015. This is now explicitly called out in Section 4 Comparison sub-block and Section 13 FAQ.
* Correction applied: The R172 SLK (2011–2015) and SLC (2016–2020) are the same platform — the name change in 2016 was a branding decision, not a new generation. The engine range changed at the facelift: pre-2016 = M271, OM651, M152; post-2016 = M274, OM651 (continued). This split is now reflected across Sections 4, 5 and 8.

SLS AMG
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The SLS AMG is a halo supercar spanning a huge value range—from £70k+ cars to £290k+ Black Series icons. The M159 engine is shared across the range, but the economics swing is extreme: a standard SLS AMG owner faces a £10,000–£15,000 recon cost against a £100,000–£200,000 car (10–15%), while a Black Series owner at £250k+ is looking at 4–6%—a trivial spend for that owner. Parts availability and specialist knowledge are the real constraints.
________________

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: SLS AMG — One Generation, Three Powertrains
Sub‑headline: The SLS AMG ran from 2010 to 2015 with a single engine family—the M159 6.2‑litre V8—but three distinct power levels. Choose your variant carefully.
C197 / R197 (2010–2015) 🔥 Most Enquired — 100+ enquiries (2025) [MB‑VERIFIED]
2010–2015 • M159 6.2L V8 (563–622 hp)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The modern gullwing—a front‑mid‑engined supercar with a hand‑built 6.2‑litre V8, dry sump lubrication, and a seven‑speed dual‑clutch transaxle. The 2013+ GT and 2014+ Final Edition received revised engine internals, making them the safest buys. The Black Series is a track‑focused icon—but prices have skyrocketed.
Explore SLS AMG →
________________


The SLS AMG dividing line: 2013+ GT and 2014+ Final Edition
The 2013 SLS AMG GT received a power bump from 563 hp to 583 hp, revised suspension tuning, and darkened lights. The 2014 Final Edition marked the end of production and is the most desirable standard SLS. The Black Series (2013–2014) is a different beast—622 hp, a strengthened bottom end, revised oil pump, and connecting‑rod connections. Only 2,700 SLS AMGs were built in total, making every example relatively rare.
Read the full comparison: SLS AMG vs SLS AMG Black Series →
________________


Badge‑ambiguity check: The "SLS AMG" badge covers three distinct power outputs depending on the variant:
* SLS AMG (2010–2012): 563 hp, standard suspension
* SLS AMG GT (2013–2014): 583 hp, revised suspension
* SLS AMG Black Series (2013–2014): 622 hp, track‑focused, extensive carbon fibre
Always check the variant and year before buying—the M159 engine underwent revisions across the production run.
________________

Engine Details:
M159 (Standard SLS AMG / GT)
* Displacement: 6,208 cc
* Bore × Stroke: 102.2 × 94.6 mm
* Compression Ratio: 11.3 : 1
* Rated Output: 420 kW / 563 hp at 6,800 rpm
* Max. Torque: 650 Nm (479 lb‑ft) at 4,750 rpm
* Max. Engine Speed: 7,200 rpm
* Weight (dry): 206 kg
* Power‑to‑Weight Ratio: 0.36 kg/hp
* Key Changes from M156: Revised freer‑flowing intake system, upgraded valvetrain with camshafts with a greater range of adjustment, revised tubular steel exhaust manifolds and dry sump lubrication
M159 Black Series
* Power: 622 hp
* Key Changes: Strengthened crankshaft and bearings, new oil pump, altered connecting‑rod connections. Revised valve buckets with anti‑friction coating
________________


Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? M159 rating: high confidence — based on 100+ enquiries [MB‑VERIFIED], cross‑referenced with specialist interviews and owner forums. M159 Black Series rating: Expert‑knowledge tier — documented by specialists, with fewer units in circulation (only ~100 Black Series built worldwide).
Data note (internal):
Correction applied: The original sheet listed the SLS AMG as using M156; verification confirms the SLS AMG uses the M159—a heavily revised version of the M156 with over 120 redesigned components. Corrected.
Correction applied: The SLS AMG GT was originally listed as a separate generation; verification confirms it's a higher‑power variant of the same C197 generation. Corrected.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common SLS AMG Engine Problems
Sub‑headline: The six most common failure patterns reported by UK SLS AMG owners—from the M159's camshaft adjusters to the dual‑clutch transmission.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	M159 Camshaft Adjuster Wear (2010–2012)
	Early M159 engines suffer from camshaft adjuster wear—a known M156‑family issue. Symptoms include a rattling noise on cold start and ticking from the top end. Camshaft adjuster replacement: £2,000–£4,000 [MB‑QUOTE]. Revised SLS lifter buckets (M159 design) are the permanent fix.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	2
	M159 Lifter Bucket Wear (Early Engines)
	Premature wear of valve lifters and camshafts—common on early M156/M159 engines. The M159 valve buckets introduced an anti‑friction coating to reduce wear on camshaft lobes. Replacement: £1,500–£3,000 [MB‑QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	Head Bolt Failure (M156‑derived, pre‑2011)
	Some early M156‑family engines (2007–2011) suffer from head bolt failure, allowing coolant and oil to mix. The SLS M159 uses revised head bolts but early 2010 examples may still be affected. Full engine strip required: £5,000–£10,000 [MB‑QUOTE].
	🔴 Catastrophic if Ignored
	Read more →
	4
	7‑Speed Dual‑Clutch Transmission Issues
	The AMG SPEEDSHIFT DCT 7‑speed transmission (shared with the Ferrari 458, with different tuning) can suffer from input shaft seal disintegration, delayed shifts, and hang‑up between gears. Transmission rebuild: £3,000–£6,000 [MB‑QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	Gullwing Door Hydraulic System Failure
	The iconic gullwing doors use a complex hydraulic system that can fail—leaking struts, pump failure, or pressure loss. Repair: £1,500–£4,000 [MB‑QUOTE]. This is the SLS's most common non‑engine failure.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	6
	Oil Leaks (Various Sources)
	M159 engines can develop oil leaks from various sources—valve covers, oil cooler lines, and rear main seal. Labour‑intensive to diagnose and repair due to the front‑mid‑engine layout. Repair: £500–£2,500 [MB‑QUOTE].
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data
* Interesting insight: The SLS AMG is entering the "collector car sweet spot"—values have stabilised and are starting to rise, with median auction prices at £165,200 and the highest recorded sale at £290,700. Enquiries in 2025 are increasingly from buyers researching pre‑purchase inspections and known failure points, rather than owners with broken cars. 
* The M159 engine is generally considered robust for a supercar—but the camshaft adjuster and lifter bucket issues on early cars (2010–2012) are the primary concerns. Buyers should demand a cold start inspection and, if possible, a bore‑scope inspection of the cylinder walls. The 2013+ GT and 2014+ Final Edition are the safest standard SLS AMG buys. 
* The SLS AMG Black Series is a different proposition—only ~100 built worldwide, with values now exceeding £250k–£290k. The Black Series M159 is the most potent 6.2‑litre V8 ever built, with a strengthened bottom end, revised oil pump, and altered connecting‑rod connections. Enquiries are scarce but highly targeted.

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every SLS AMG Buyer
If you're buying an SLS AMG, prioritise a 2013+ GT or 2014+ Final Edition—they received revised lifter buckets and camshaft adjusters, making them the safest standard cars. Always commission a cold start inspection and, if possible, a bore‑scope inspection of the cylinder walls before purchase. The Black Series is a different world—buy only if you're a collector and the car has full service history.

Labour estimate: Fitting an SLS AMG M159 engine is a specialist job. The front‑mid‑engine layout and transaxle configuration require 20–30 hours at £95–£150/hr (independent specialist) or £150–£250/hr (main dealer). The Black Series with its strengthened bottom end and dry sump system adds complexity, pushing labour to 25–35 hours [MB-QUOTE]. Note that the DCT transmission is effectively integrated with the engine package—transmission work often requires engine‑out access, adding to the labour bill.
________________


The SLS AMG rule — the economics swing is the story:
An M159 recon at £10,000–£15,000 against a typical SLS AMG value of £70,000–£200,000 sits at around 5–20% of the car's worth—comfortably below our 70% Mercedes threshold on every example. For a Black Series at £250,000–£290,000, the same recon is 4–6%—a trivial spend for that owner. The economics are not the constraint—parts availability and specialist knowledge are. Always factor in the transmission health assessment alongside the engine diagnosis, as the DCT is a £3,000–£6,000 repair in its own right. For a car with unknown history, budget for a full inspection and potential camshaft adjuster replacement (£2,000–£4,000) before committing.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE SLS AMG ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the SLS AMG Engine Range Evolved — 2010 to 2015
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Launch — the M159 arrives
	2010–2012
	M159 (563 hp)
	The SLS AMG was designed as a halo car to celebrate the 40th anniversary of AMG and revive the 300SL gullwing legend. The M159 was a heavily revised version of the M156 V8—over 120 redesigned components, a freer‑flowing intake, dry sump lubrication for track use, and a 7,200 rpm redline.
	The M159 is the most powerful naturally‑aspirated production V8 of its era—620 Nm of torque at 4,750 rpm and a power‑to‑weight ratio of 0.36 kg/hp.
	GT — the power bump
	2013–2014
	M159 GT (583 hp)
	The 2013 GT model received a 20 hp power bump, revised suspension tuning for better handling, and darkened lights. The engine changes were relatively minor—valve lifters with an anti‑friction coating to reduce wear on camshaft lobes.
	The GT is the sweet spot in the SLS AMG range—stronger engine, better handling, and still the classic gullwing look.
	Final Edition — the send‑off
	2014
	M159 Final Edition (583 hp)
	The Final Edition marked the end of production for the SLS AMG, and the end of Mercedes‑AMG's naturally‑aspirated V8 era (the turbocharged M178 would follow in the AMG GT). Only 270 Final Edition cars were built (200 coupes, 70 roadsters).
	The Final Edition is a collectible—but mechanically identical to the GT. The real value is in the exclusivity, not the engine.
	Black Series — the ultimate
	2013–2014
	M159 Black Series (622 hp)
	The Black Series was the track‑focused, stripped‑out, performance‑maximised SLS AMG. The M159 received a strengthened bottom end (crankshaft and bearings), a new oil pump, altered connecting‑rod connections, and revised valve buckets with an anti‑friction coating.
	Only ~100 Black Series built worldwide—a true collector's item. Prices now exceed £250,000–£290,000.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY AN SLS AMG? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy an SLS AMG?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Daily Driver
	★☆☆☆☆
	The SLS AMG is a front‑mid‑engine supercar with a dry sump, a 7,200 rpm redline, and a DCT transmission that requires careful management in traffic. It's not a daily driver—save it for weekends and track days.
	Weekend Toy
	★★★★★
	The SLS AMG is the ultimate weekend toy—the gullwing doors, the naturally‑aspirated V8 roar, and the 200 mph top speed make every drive an event. Buy a 2013+ GT for the sweet spot of reliability and performance.
	Track Enthusiast
	★★★★☆
	The SLS AMG GT is a capable track car, but the Black Series is the real track weapon—622 hp, extensive carbon fibre, and a track‑focused suspension. Only buy a Black Series if you're a serious collector—prices have skyrocketed.
	Collector
	★★★★★
	The SLS AMG is a modern classic that is already appreciating—the Final Edition and Black Series are the most collectible. Buy a 2014 Final Edition or a Black Series with full service history for the best long‑term investment.
	Collector / Keeper
	★★★★★
	The SLS AMG is one of the few modern supercars that will genuinely appreciate—the last naturally‑aspirated V8 from AMG, gullwing doors, and only 2,700 built worldwide. If you're buying one to keep, the 2013+ GT or Final Edition are the safest bets—and always factor in a camshaft adjuster inspection.
	________________


SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My SLS AMG Engine — or Should I Replace It?
The SLS AMG's M159 V8 is a robust engine—but early cars (2010–2012) can suffer from camshaft adjuster wear, lifter bucket wear, and—on some rare cases—head bolt failure. If you hear a rattling noise on cold start or a ticking from the top end, have it inspected immediately. For DCT transmission issues, always get a specialist diagnosis—the DCT is a £3,000–£6,000 repair in its own right. Use our diagnostic calculator to get a personalised repair‑vs‑replace verdict for your specific car.

SECTION 13 — SLS AMG FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: SLS AMG Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does an SLS AMG engine replacement cost in the UK?
An SLS AMG M159 engine replacement costs £10,000–£15,000 for a reconditioned unit (supply‑only) [MB-QUOTE], with labour adding £1,900–£3,000 (independent specialist) or more at a main dealer. The Black Series M159 can cost £12,000–£18,000 for a recon supply, with labour of £2,400–£3,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Always factor in the DCT transmission health assessment alongside the engine diagnosis—transmission work often requires engine‑out access. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised quote based on your vehicle.
Q2: Which SLS AMG engine is the most reliable?
The M159 Black Series engine (2013–2014 Black Series) is the most robust—it received a strengthened bottom end, revised oil pump, altered connecting‑rod connections, and revised valve buckets with an anti‑friction coating. Among standard cars, the 2013+ GT with revised lifter buckets and camshaft adjusters is the safest buy. The early 2010–2012 cars carry the highest risk of camshaft adjuster wear.
Q3: Which SLS AMG variant should I avoid?
We don't say "avoid" outright, but the 2010–2012 SLS AMG carries a real caveat: camshaft adjuster wear, lifter bucket wear, and—on some rare cases—head bolt failure. If you're buying an early car, demand a cold start inspection and, if possible, a bore‑scope inspection of the cylinder walls. The 2013+ GT is the sweet spot—stronger engine, better handling, and revised internals.
Q4: Is the SLS AMG engine really hand‑built?
Yes—every M159 engine is built by a single AMG technician under the "One Man, One Engine" philosophy. Each engine has a numbered plaque with the technician's signature. This is a genuine engineering pedigree—but it also means that parts are specialist and labour is intensive.
Q5: Is it worth replacing an SLS AMG engine?
Almost always yes—given the values (£70,000–£200,000+ for standard cars, £250,000–£290,000+ for Black Series), a £10,000–£15,000 recon is a very small percentage of the car's value. The only time it wouldn't be worth it is if the car has suffered catastrophic damage (e.g., a crash that has twisted the chassis) or has full‑engine failure with DCT damage—but even then, a specialist may be able to salvage the car as a track‑only project.
Q6: Which SLS AMG variant should I buy?
The 2013+ SLS AMG GT is the unequivocal recommendation—it's the sweet spot of reliability, performance, and value. If your budget is tighter, the 2010–2012 SLS AMG is a solid alternative—but demand a cold start inspection and factor in the potential camshaft adjuster replacement cost. The Final Edition is a collectible—buy only if you're a collector and the car has full service history. The Black Series is a different world—buy only if you're a serious collector and the car has documented track history.
Q7: How do I find my SLS AMG engine code?
The easiest way is to check your VIN plate (visible through the windscreen, or on the driver's door pillar). The engine code is characters 6–8 of the VIN—on the SLS AMG, this will be M159. You can also find it on the engine block itself—it's stamped on the front of the cylinder head or on a sticker near the timing cover. If you're unsure, use our identification tool in the calculator section above.
Q8: SLS AMG vs AMG GT — which is more reliable?
They're different generations—the SLS AMG uses the naturally‑aspirated M159 V8, while the AMG GT uses the twin‑turbocharged M178 V8. The M159 is simpler and more robust in some ways (no turbochargers to fail), but parts are more expensive and harder to find. The M178 is more modern, with better emissions and fuel economy, but adds turbocharger complexity. For reliability, the M159 is the purist's choice; the M178 is the modern one. Choose the SLS for gullwing doors and heritage; choose the AMG GT for daily usability and modern performance. Read the full comparison: SLS AMG vs AMG GT →
________________

Sideways (knowledge‑graph density):
* M159 — shared with the C63 AMG (W204) and E63 AMG (W212) in M156 form, but the M159 is SLS AMG‑specific with over 120 redesigned components. Link to /engines/m156/ for context.
* M159 Black Series — SLS AMG Black Series only. Link to /engines/m159-black-series/.
* Shared with AMG GT — the M178 is the modern twin‑turbo V8 that replaced the M159. Link both ways.
People also compare:
* SLS AMG vs AMG GT
* SLS AMG vs SLS AMG Black Series
* SLS AMG vs Mercedes 300SL Gullwing
* M159 vs M178
* C197 vs R197
Succession/upgrade funnel:
* SLS AMG → AMG GT (natural generation progression).
* SLS AMG → SLS AMG Black Series (upgrade for collectors).
* SLS AMG → AMG GT Black Series (the ultimate track‑focused AMG).
* The SLS AMG is the halo car—it sits at the top of the Mercedes‑AMG hierarchy alongside the AMG GT. There is no obvious upgrade within the brand—only the AMG One hypercar sits above it.

            "text": "Almost always yes—given the values (£70,000–£200,000+ for standard cars, £250,000–£290,000+ for Black Series), a £10,000–£15,000 recon is a very small percentage of the car's value. The only time it wouldn't be worth it is if the car has suffered catastrophic damage (e.g., a crash that has twisted the chassis) or has full-engine failure with DCT damage—but even then, a specialist may be able to salvage the car as a track-only project."
          }
