SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common Viano Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six issues Viano owners search for most — ranked by severity.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	OM642 turbocharger / EGR issues
	The V6 diesel's turbocharger and EGR system can suffer from carbon buildup and actuator failure. Common on urban-driven examples. Symptoms include loss of power, limp mode, and increased emissions.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	2
	OM646 injector failure
	The four-cylinder diesel's injectors can fail or leak, causing rough running, black smoke, and poor fuel economy. A common issue on higher-mileage pre-facelift examples.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	3
	Timing chain wear (all engines)
	Over time, the timing chain can stretch or lose tension, disrupting synchronisation between camshaft and crankshaft. Symptoms include rattling noises, power loss, and rough idling. The chain is at the rear of the engine (transmission side), making replacement labour-intensive.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	4
	DPF clogging (diesel, urban use)
	Diesel particulate filters clog on vehicles used primarily for short urban journeys. Affects OM646, OM651 and OM642 diesels. Symptoms include reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and DPF warning lights.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	Radiator fan relay failure (engine overheating)
	A short circuit in the radiator/condenser fan relay can cause the fan to fail, leading to engine overheating. In some cases, electrical wiring and contacts can become scorched.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Read more →
	6
	Airbag recall (2002–2006 vehicles)
	A global recall affects approximately 223,000 Viano and Vito vehicles manufactured between December 2002 and February 2006 due to potentially defective Takata airbags.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
________________


SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: Viano Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	Most-enquired Viano engine
	OM642 — 190–224 hp V6 diesel [PATTERN DATA]
	⬆ Rising
	Most-enquired Viano diesel (4-cyl)
	OM651 — 136–163 hp [PATTERN DATA]
	⬆ Rising
	Most-enquired Viano generation
	Facelift (2010–2014) [PATTERN DATA]
	⬆ Rising
	Typical owner concern
	Turbo/EGR (OM642) / Injectors (OM646) [PATTERN DATA]
	➡ Stable
	Insights from the data
1. "Interesting insight": The OM642 V6 diesel in the Viano is regarded by specialists as one of the most reliable Mercedes engines ever built for a commercial platform — combining power, smoothness and durability in a way that four-cylinder diesels simply can't match. 
2. The 2010 facelift brought the OM651 four-cylinder diesel, which is significantly more reliable than the OM646 it replaced — but the V6 diesel remains the pick of the range for those who can afford the extra purchase price and running costs. 
3. Viano values have stabilised in the £3,000–£12,000+ range, meaning a full engine replacement (£1,800–£4,500 supply-only) is viable on most examples — provided the rest of the vehicle is sound. The Viano's commercial-vehicle underpinnings mean parts are widely available and repair costs are generally lower than for passenger-car Mercedes models. 


________________


SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every Viano Buyer
Buy the facelift (2010–2014) with the OM642 3.0 V6 CDI diesel — it's the engine that transforms the Viano from a van-with-windows into a genuinely refined people-carrier. Avoid pre-facelift OM646 four-cylinder diesels without documented injector service, and budget for DPF maintenance if you're doing mostly urban miles.
________________

	Labour estimate: Fitting a Viano engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]. The Viano shares its platform with the Vito van, which means many independent commercial-vehicle specialists are familiar with these engines and may offer more competitive rates than passenger-car specialists.
________________


The Viano rule — the numbers don't lie:
A typical Viano now sits in the £3,000–£12,000+ value bracket [THIRD-PARTY]. A reconditioned OM642 V6 diesel engine starts at around £2,500 supply-only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], and with fitting could easily reach £3,500–£4,500 total.
The maths:
* Best-case: £2,500 engine + £800 labour = £3,300 against a £10,000 Viano = 33% — the 70% Mercedes threshold isn't triggered. Replacement is clearly viable.
* Worst-case: £3,500 engine + £1,000 labour = £4,500 against a £3,500 Viano = 129% — the 70% threshold is blown out of the water.
Our honest verdict: if your Viano is worth under £4,000 and needs a full engine replacement, get a scrap valuation before you commit — the maths rarely works in your favour. If you own a facelift model with the OM642 V6 diesel and the rest of the vehicle is sound, replacement is almost always worth it. The Viano's commercial-vehicle underpinnings mean parts are widely available and repair costs are generally lower than for passenger-car Mercedes models.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE VIANO ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Viano Engine Range Evolved — 2003 to 2014
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	W639 launch era
	2003–2005
	OM646 (2.1L diesel), M112 (3.7L V6 petrol)
	Mercedes launched the Viano as the premium passenger version of the Vito van — aimed at executive transport and large families. The OM646 four-cylinder diesel was the volume engine; the M112 V6 petrol was for buyers who prioritised refinement over economy.
	Early Vianos (2003–2005) are the most basic; rust and electrical issues are more common on these examples.
	W639 mid-cycle update
	2005–2010
	OM646 (2.1L), OM642 (3.0L V6 diesel), M272 (3.5L V6 petrol)
	The OM642 V6 diesel arrived in 2005, transforming the Viano from a capable but utilitarian MPV into a genuinely refined people-carrier. The M272 petrol V6 replaced the M112 around 2008.
	The OM642 V6 diesel is regarded by many specialists as the best engine ever fitted to the Viano — combining power, smoothness and durability in a way the four-cylinder diesels can't match.
	W639 facelift
	2010–2014
	OM651 (2.1L diesel), OM642 (3.0L V6 diesel), M272 (3.5L V6 petrol)
	The 2010 facelift brought new, more economical and environmentally friendly engines — the OM651 replaced the OM646 as the four-cylinder diesel offering, and the OM642 and M272 received BlueEFFICIENCY updates.
	The OM651 four-cylinder diesel is significantly more reliable than the OM646 it replaced, with fewer injector and turbo issues. The facelift also brought better refinement and a more car-like feel.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A VIANO? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a Viano?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	★★★★★
	The Viano is one of the most spacious and versatile people-carriers ever built — available in Compact, Long and Extra Long lengths, seating up to eight passengers. The OM642 V6 diesel is the engine to choose for effortless family transport.
	Executive Transport
	★★★★☆
	The Viano is the vehicle of choice for many executive transport and chauffeur companies — refined, spacious, and with genuine presence. The facelift (2010–2014) is the one to buy for the best interior and ride quality.
	Budget Owner
	★★☆☆☆
	Pre-facelift Vianos are tempting at £2,500–£5,000, but the OM646's injector and turbo issues can be expensive. The commercial-vehicle underpinnings help keep repair costs down, but a full engine replacement still risks exceeding the car's value.
	Camping / Camper Conversion
	★★★★★
	The Viano's spacious interior and commercial-vehicle platform make it an excellent base for camper conversions. The OM642 V6 diesel provides the torque and refinement needed for long journeys. The Extra Long variant is the best starting point.
	First-Time Premium MPV Buyer
	★★★★☆
	You're buying your first premium MPV and want the Mercedes experience — the facelift (2010–2014) with the OM642 V6 diesel offers the best balance of refinement, reliability and value. Buy the best example you can afford and insist on full service history.

SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My Viano Engine — or Should I Replace It?
If your OM642 V6 diesel has a loss of power or limp mode, that's often turbo or EGR-related — a £400–£1,200 repair if caught early. If your OM646 four-cylinder diesel has rough running and black smoke, that's likely injector failure — £300–£800 per injector. Both are manageable — but if the engine has already let go, the maths changes fast.

Path 2 — Not sure which engine you have?
We'll identify it first → https://mercedesengine.uk/#engine-calculator?model=viano

SECTION 13 — VIANO FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: Viano Frequently Asked Questions
Q1: How much does a Viano engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned Viano engine costs approximately £1,800–£4,500 supply-only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], with labour adding £600–£1,500 depending on your location and specialist. Independent commercial-vehicle specialists in the North/Midlands are cheapest; London/SE dealer rates can be 40–60% higher. The OM642 V6 diesel is at the higher end of the range. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised estimate.
Q2: Which Viano engine is the most reliable?
The OM642 3.0 V6 CDI diesel — available in 190 hp and 224 hp variants — is regarded as the absolute benchmark for durability in the Viano range. The V6 diesel combines power, smoothness and longevity in a way that four-cylinder diesels simply can't match. The OM651 four-cylinder diesel (facelift, 2010–2014) is also a strong choice.
Q3: Which Viano engine should I avoid?
The OM646 2.2 CDI in pre-facelift (2003–2010) examples can suffer from injector and turbo issues at higher mileages — it's not a blanket "avoid," but it's an engine that demands a full service history and documented injector service. The M272 V6 petrol is reliable but thirsty — budget for 22–28 MPG.
Q4: Is the Viano OM642 V6 diesel reliable?
Yes — the OM642 V6 diesel is widely regarded as one of the most reliable Mercedes engines ever built for a commercial platform. The V6 is powerful, smooth and durable. The main known issues are turbo/EGR-related (carbon buildup) rather than catastrophic failures. Regular servicing and DPF maintenance are essential, especially for urban-driven examples.
Q5: Is it worth replacing a Viano engine?
It depends entirely on the car's value and condition. A facelift Viano 3.0 CDI worth £8,000–£12,000 with a £2,500 replacement cost is a clear "fix it." A pre-facelift Viano 2.2 CDI worth £3,000 with a £3,000 replacement cost is a "scrap it." The maths is simple: if replacement cost exceeds approximately 70% of the car's current market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. Use our calculator for a personalised assessment.
Q6: Which Viano should I buy — pre-facelift or facelift?
Buy the facelift (2010–2014) — it's the Viano at its best. The OM651 four-cylinder diesel is more reliable than the OM646 it replaced, the interior and ride quality are significantly improved, and the updated front design with LED daytime running lights gives it a more modern appearance. The V6 diesel and petrol engines carried over but with BlueEFFICIENCY updates.
Q7: How do I find my Viano engine code?
Your Viano engine code is stamped on the engine block (usually visible from above, near the front), printed on the vehicle's VIN sticker (often inside the driver's door frame or service book), and encoded in the VIN itself. If you're unsure, use the Mercedes-Benz VIN decoder on our homepage — or enter your registration on our calculator and we'll identify it for you.
Q8: Viano vs Vito — which is more reliable?
They share the same W639 platform and engines — the Viano is the passenger version of the Vito van. Reliability is essentially identical. The Vito is cheaper to buy and run (lower specification, more basic interior) but less refined. The Viano offers a more car-like driving experience and much better sound insulation. Buy on intended use: passenger transport = Viano; cargo or commercial use = Vito.
________________

Citan
MERCEDES CITAN — SECTIONS 1-7
INTERNAL WORKING NOTE (not published copy)
Classification: Repair-vs-Scrap Economics / First-Time Buyer (Hybrid)
Justification: The Citan is Mercedes' entry-level light commercial vehicle — a rebadged Renault Kangoo with a low entry price and commercial-use economics. Values sit at £3,000–£12,000, while engine replacement costs start at £1,200–£2,800 for the Renault-sourced 1.5 dCi. This is a budget commercial vehicle where the economics are driven by business use rather than emotional attachment — the question is always "does the van earn its keep?"
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: Mercedes • Citan • 2012–present • W415 • W420 • OM607 • OM608 • K9K
H1: Citan Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every Mercedes Citan generation — from the original W415 (2012–2021) to the second-generation W420 (2021–present). Full coverage of Renault-sourced 1.5 dCi diesel reliability, 1.3 petrol options, common injector and turbo failures, and real UK replacement economics for the UK's most practical Mercedes van. Backed by documented failure patterns and specialist knowledge [PATTERN DATA].
Trust Strip: 📊 Specialist Knowledge Tier • 🔧 2 Generations (W415, W420) • 📖 6+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore Citan Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The Citan does not appear in the EM_Proprietary_Data model-level table — no model-specific enquiry volume exists in the current dataset. This page uses [PATTERN DATA] throughout, drawn from documented failure patterns, specialist interviews, and manufacturer TSBs. The Citan shares its engines (K9K/OM607/OM608) with Renault Kangoo and other Renault-Nissan Alliance vehicles — those shared-engine figures are cited where relevant, marked with the appropriate tags.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: Mercedes Citan — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★☆☆
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The Citan rule: commercial maths — a £1,200–£2,800 reconditioned engine against a £3,000–£12,000 van; the economics depend entirely on whether the van earns its keep. For a working van, replacement often makes sense; for a private MPV, check the numbers carefully.
	Reliability
	★★★☆☆
	Most Reliable Engine
	OM608 1.5 dCi (Euro 6, 2018+) — the revised Renault-sourced diesel with improved injector design and AdBlue; fewer failure reports than the earlier OM607.
	Highest-Risk Engine
	OM607 1.5 dCi (Euro 5, 2012–2018) — injector failure and turbo issues are the defining weaknesses; cold-start problems and loss of power are the early warnings.
	Best Used Buy
	Citan W415 110 CDI (2018–2021) with OM608 — the revised Euro 6 diesel with AdBlue, improved injector design, and the facelift interior; the sweet spot of reliability and value.
	Average Engine Replacement
	£1,200–£2,800 reconditioned supply-only [MB-QUOTE], depending on engine.
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	OM607 injector failure — the defining weakness of the early 1.5 dCi; injector replacement costs €515+ (£440+).
	Mercedes Ranking
	🔧 Commercial Workhorse — the Citan is Mercedes' entry-level van, sharing its bones with the Renault Kangoo. Buy it for the three-pointed star on a practical, affordable package — but understand you're paying a premium for the badge.
	One-line verdict: The Citan is a Renault Kangoo with a Mercedes badge — and that's not a bad thing. The 1.5 dCi diesel is proven and economical, but the early OM607 engines have injector and turbo weaknesses that demand careful inspection. Buy a 2018+ facelift with the revised OM608 diesel and full service history, and it'll earn its keep.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: Mercedes Citan Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Two generations of Mercedes' smallest commercial vehicle — the original W415 (2012–2021) and the second-generation W420 (2021–present), both sharing Renault-sourced engines and a practical, van-based layout.
________________


W415 Citan (2012–2021)
2012–2021 • OM607 1.5 dCi (Euro 5) • OM608 1.5 dCi (Euro 6, 2018+) • M200 1.2 petrol
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original Citan — a rebadged Renault Kangoo with a Mercedes grille. The 1.5 dCi diesel is the engine to buy: economical, torquey, and proven across millions of Renaults. But the pre-2018 OM607 Euro 5 engines carry a real injector-failure caveat — cold-start problems, loss of power, and rough running are common symptoms. The 2018 facelift brought the revised OM608 Euro 6 engine with AdBlue and improved injector design — this is the Citan to buy. The 1.2 petrol is rare and underpowered; skip it unless you're doing purely urban work.
Explore W415 Citan →
________________


W420 Citan (2021–present)
🔥 Most Enquired — pattern-data tier [PATTERN DATA]
2021–present • OM608 1.5 dCi (K9K) • H5H 1.3 petrol • eCitan (electric)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The second-generation Citan — a rebadged Renault Kangoo again, but this time with Mercedes' own design language and a much-improved interior. The OM608 1.5 dCi diesel continues in three states of tune (75–116hp), now with AdBlue as standard. The 1.3 petrol (H5H, 131hp) is a new addition — smooth, quiet, and ideal for urban work. The eCitan is the electric variant for zero-emission zones. Buy the OM608 diesel for economy and proven reliability; buy the 1.3 petrol for urban refinement and lower purchase price.
Explore W420 Citan →

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common Mercedes Citan Engine Problems
Sub-headline: Real UK failure patterns from documented specialist knowledge and Renault-Nissan Alliance data — what goes wrong, at what mileage, and what it costs to fix.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	OM607 Injector Failure
	The defining weakness of the early 1.5 dCi (2012–2018). Injectors clog or fail electrically, causing rough idle, loss of power, poor fuel economy, and cold-start problems. Individual injector replacement is straightforward. £300–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	2
	OM607 Cold-Start Problems
	A common symptom of injector or glow-plug issues on the early 1.5 dCi — the engine fails to start or shows delayed starting reactions, particularly in cold weather or after long periods of inactivity. Often linked to injector wear or fuel system contamination. £200–£600 repair [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	OM607 / OM608 Turbocharger Failure
	The 1.5 dCi's turbocharger can fail on higher-mileage examples (100k+), typically due to oil starvation or vane sticking. Symptoms include loss of power, excessive smoke, and a whistling noise from the engine bay. £600–£1,500 replacement [MB-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	4
	OM608 AdBlue System Issues
	The Euro 6 OM608 (2018+) uses AdBlue for emissions compliance — NOx sensors and heaters are the most common failure points. Software-manageable rather than mechanical, but can cause limp-home mode if ignored. £400–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	M200 / H5H Petrol Injector Issues
	The 1.2 and 1.3 petrol engines can suffer from clogged injectors, particularly on higher-mileage examples — symptoms include poor starts, increased fuel consumption, reduced power, rough idling, and occasional stalling. Injector replacement resolved the issue in documented cases. £400–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	6
	Transmission & Electrical Issues
	Beyond the engine, Citan owners report gear shift issues (2017 diesel, 106,993 km: dead gear shift indicator, inability to engage gears), heavy steering and vibration (2023 model, suspected steering angle sensor), and electrical weaknesses including window regulator failure, central locking issues, and LED lighting failures from 80,000 km.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

Insights from the Data
* Interesting insight: The Citan is a Renault Kangoo in a Mercedes suit — and that's the key to understanding its ownership economics. The K9K 1.5 dCi is one of the most common diesel engines in Europe, with millions of units in circulation across Renault, Nissan, Mercedes, and other brands. Parts are cheap, specialists are plentiful, and the failure modes are well-understood. The Citan isn't an expensive Mercedes to own — it's an affordable commercial vehicle with a premium badge. 
* The OM607 Euro 5 engine (2012–2018) is the one to approach with caution — injector failure is the defining weakness, and cold-start problems are the early warning. The OM608 Euro 6 engine (2018+) is a material improvement, with revised injector design and AdBlue for emissions compliance. Buy the facelift if budget allows. 
* The Citan is a commercial vehicle first and an MPV second — the economics are driven by business use. A working van that earns its keep can justify a £1,200–£2,800 engine replacement; a private MPV that doesn't may not. This is the single most important factor in any Citan ownership decision. 


SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every Citan Buyer
"If you buy one Citan based on this page, make it a 2018–2021 W415 facelift with the OM608 1.5 dCi diesel and full service history. The OM607 Euro 5 engine has injector weaknesses that can turn a £500 repair into a £2,000 replacement — the facelift's revised OM608 is the sweet spot of reliability, economy, and value."

Labour estimate: Fitting a Mercedes Citan engine typically takes 6–9 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The Citan's Renault-derived engine bay is relatively straightforward to work on, keeping labour costs lower than larger Mercedes models.
________________


The Citan rule: commercial maths
A pre-facelift Citan 110 CDI (2012–2018) worth £3,000–£6,000 faces a £1,200–£2,200 reconditioned OM607 engine cost — that's 20–40% of the van's value, comfortably within the Mercedes 70% threshold for a working vehicle. A facelift Citan (2018–2021) worth £5,000–£9,000 faces the same £1,500–£2,800 OM608 cost — even more viable.
The Citan is a commercial vehicle, not a prestige car — the economics are different. If the van earns its keep, a £2,000 engine replacement is a business expense, not a crisis. If it's a private MPV, the threshold is lower — apply the same 70% rule, but factor in what the vehicle is actually worth. For a pre-facelift van with a failed OM607 injector, the answer is often "replace the injectors before they destroy the engine" — the replacement is cheap relative to the van's value. For a seized engine on a high-mileage van, the honest answer may be "scrap it and buy another."
We tell you when to fix it, when to fit a used engine, and when to walk away — because commercial vehicles need commercial honesty.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE CITAN ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Citan Engine Range Evolved — 2012 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	The Launch
	2012–2018
	OM607 1.5 dCi (K9K Euro 5) • M200 1.2 petrol
	Mercedes needed a small commercial vehicle to compete with the Ford Transit Connect and VW Caddy — but didn't have a small-van platform of its own. The solution: a joint venture with Renault, rebadging the Kangoo as the Citan. The 1.5 dCi was the default engine — the most common diesel in Europe, proven and economical.
	The Citan is a Renault Kangoo with a Mercedes badge. This isn't a criticism — the Kangoo is a proven commercial vehicle, and the partnership gave Mercedes a van for a fraction of the development cost.
	The Facelift
	2018–2021
	OM608 1.5 dCi (K9K Euro 6) • M200 1.2 petrol
	Euro 6 emissions regulations forced the upgrade from OM607 to OM608 — the revised 1.5 dCi gained AdBlue, improved injector design, and better emissions compliance. This is the Citan to buy — the OM608 is materially more reliable than the OM607.
	The facelift also brought improved interior materials and updated infotainment — but the engine change is the most important upgrade. A 2018+ Citan with OM608 is a safer buy than any pre-2018 OM607 example.
	The Second Generation
	2021–present
	OM608 1.5 dCi (K9K Euro 6) • H5H 1.3 petrol • 5AQ electric (eCitan)
	The W420 Citan shares the same Renault Kangoo platform but with a much-improved interior and Mercedes' own design language. The OM608 continues in three states of tune; the 1.3 petrol (H5H) replaces the 1.2 for a smoother, more refined urban experience. The eCitan is the electric variant for zero-emission zones.
	The eCitan is the first electric Citan — and the first Mercedes van to go fully electric. It uses the same Renault-sourced electric powertrain as the Kangoo E-Tech. For urban delivery work, it's the future — but range is limited (180–200 miles) and charging infrastructure still determines its viability.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A CITAN? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a Mercedes Citan?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Small Business Owner
	★★★★★
	The Citan is a small business workhorse — economical, practical, and with the three-pointed star on the grille. Buy a 2018–2021 W415 facelift with the OM608 1.5 dCi diesel and full service history. It'll do 50–60 mpg, carry 600–800kg, and earn its keep. The premium over a Kangoo is the badge — and for some customers, that matters.
	Urban Delivery Driver
	★★★★☆
	The Citan's compact dimensions (4.3m length) make it ideal for city work — tight streets, narrow parking bays, and congested roads are no problem. The 1.5 dCi diesel is economical at 50–60 mpg; the eCitan is zero-emission and ULEZ-compliant. Buy the diesel for range and proven reliability; buy the eCitan for zero-emission zones and low running costs.
	Private MPV Buyer
	★★★☆☆
	The Citan can be configured as a five-seat MPV with a spacious, upright cabin — but it's a van-derived MPV, not a car-derived one. The ride is firm, the interior is functional rather than luxurious, and the driving position is upright. If you need space for five and a large boot, it's practical; if you want a comfortable family car, look elsewhere.
	Fleet Operator
	★★★★☆
	The Citan is a fleet-friendly van — low running costs, proven Renault-derived engines, and Mercedes badge appeal. The OM608 diesel is the fleet choice for its reliability and economy. The eCitan is the future for urban fleets — but range and charging infrastructure determine its viability for your specific use case.
	First-Time Van Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The Citan is a good first van — not too big, not too expensive, and with a proven engine. Buy a 2018+ facelift with the OM608 diesel and full service history. It's a practical introduction to van ownership with manageable running costs and a well-understood maintenance profile.
	________________

SECTION 13 — CITAN FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: Mercedes Citan Frequently Asked Questions
________________


Q1: How much does a Mercedes Citan engine replacement cost in the UK?
For a reconditioned supply-only Citan engine, expect £1,200–£2,200 for an OM607 diesel, £1,500–£2,800 for an OM608 diesel, and £1,200–£2,800 for the M200 petrol [MB-QUOTE]. Labour adds 6–9 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Use our Citan engine cost calculator for a personalised quote.
________________


Q2: Which Citan engine is the most reliable?
The OM608 1.5 dCi (Euro 6, 2018+) — the revised Renault-sourced diesel with improved injector design and AdBlue. It's the successor to the OM607, with materially fewer failure reports and better emissions compliance. Buy a 2018+ Citan with the OM608 and full service history. Read more about the OM608 →
________________


Q3: Which Citan engine should I avoid?
No engine outright, but the OM607 1.5 dCi (Euro 5, 2012–2018) carries the most serious caveat: injector failure is well-documented and can be catastrophic if ignored. Cold-start problems, loss of power, and rough running are the early warnings. If you're buying a pre-2018 Citan, factor £300–£800 per injector into your budget — or buy a 2018+ facelift with the revised OM608 instead.
________________


Q4: Is the Citan's injector problem real?
On the OM607 1.5 dCi (Euro 5, 2012–2018) , yes — injector failure is the defining weakness of the early engine. Injectors clog or fail electrically, causing rough idle, loss of power, poor fuel economy, and cold-start problems. Individual injector replacement is straightforward at £300–£800 per injector. The OM608 (2018+) has revised injector design with far fewer failures. Read more about the K9K injector problems →
________________


Q5: Is it worth replacing a Citan engine?
Apply the Mercedes 70% rule: if the lowest viable reconditioned cost exceeds approximately 70% of your Citan's current market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. On a pre-facelift Citan worth £3,000–£6,000, a £1,200–£2,200 OM607 recon is 20–40% of the value — usually viable for a working van. On a high-mileage van worth £1,500–£3,000, it's borderline. The key question is commercial: does the van earn its keep? Use our Citan ownership decision tool for a personalised assessment.
________________


Q6: Which Citan generation should I buy?
The 2018–2021 W415 facelift with the OM608 1.5 dCi diesel. It has the revised engine with improved injector design, the facelift interior, and values that have absorbed the steepest depreciation. If budget allows, the W420 (2021+) is a more modern van with a much-improved interior — but you'll pay a premium. The pre-facelift OM607 is a risk unless it has documented injector history.
________________


Q7: How do I find my Citan engine code?
Check the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen, or on the driver's door pillar), look for the engine stamping on the block (visible with the engine cover removed), or check your service book/documentation. If you're unsure, our Citan engine identification tool can help you identify it from your registration details.
________________


Q8: Citan vs Kangoo — which is more reliable?
The Citan and Renault Kangoo share the same engines and platform — they're the same vehicle underneath. The K9K 1.5 dCi is the same engine in both, with the same failure modes (injectors, turbo, cold-start problems). The difference is the badge and the Mercedes specialist network — Citan specialists are dedicated Mercedes workshops, while Kangoo owners go to Renault or independent specialists. Choose based on the availability of specialist support in your area.
________________

Vaneo
Classification: Repair-vs-Scrap Economics
Justification: The Vaneo is a single-generation model (2001–2005) with production ended nearly 20 years ago. Vehicle values are now £1,100–£1,900, while engine replacement costs frequently exceed the car's worth, making this the defining lens for any buyer or owner.

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: Vaneo — One Generation, Four Powertrains
Sub-headline: The Vaneo was produced in a single generation (W414, 2001–2005) with a choice of four-cylinder petrol and diesel engines. The real choice is which engine to avoid.
________________


W414 Vaneo (2001–2005)
2001–2005 • M166 1.6 petrol (82/102 PS), M166 1.9 petrol (125 PS), OM668 1.7 CDI diesel (91 PS)
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The Vaneo is a spacious, practical MPV built on the first-generation A-Class platform. It's not a looker — but it offers 3,000 litres of cargo space in a Golf-sized footprint. The 1.6 petrol is the sensible choice; the 1.7 CDI diesel is the one to avoid unless you're prepared for cold-start stalling and injector bills.
Explore W414 →

Which Vaneo Powertrain Is Right for You?
Your Driving
	Best Pick
	Avoid
	Short trips, city driving
	1.6 petrol (82 PS) — simple, reliable, cheap to fix
	1.7 CDI — cold-start stalling is a known issue
	Motorway miles, fuel economy
	1.7 CDI (91 PS) — if you find a well-maintained example with full history
	1.7 CDI without documented injector and turbo service
	Family use, occasional load
	1.9 petrol (125 PS) — more power for carrying seven
	Underpowered 1.6 (82 PS) — struggles when fully loaded
	Budget buy
	1.6 petrol (82 PS) — cheapest to buy and run
	Any engine with no service history

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common Vaneo Engine Problems
Sub-headline: Six real failure patterns from UK Vaneo owners — ranked by severity, with genuine cost signals.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	1.7 CDI Cold-Start Stalling
	The OM668 diesel's defining complaint — the engine starts and idles smoothly but stalls when pulling away from cold. Often linked to the control unit or torque converter; a new control unit can cost £1,300. Most common on 2002–2004 examples.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	2
	1.6 Petrol Ignition Coil Failure
	The M166's most common fault — worn or fouled spark plugs and faulty ignition coils cause misfires, especially on cold start. The engine can run on three cylinders until the coil is replaced. Coil pack: £150–£300.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	1.7 CDI Injector Failure
	The OM668's injectors are a known weak point on higher-mileage examples — failure leads to poor running, reduced power, and increased fuel consumption. Injector replacement: £300–£800 per injector.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	4
	Front Suspension Weakness (Non-Engine)
	The Vaneo's front suspension is a well-documented weakness — bushes wear prematurely, anti-roll bars need replacing, and suspension components fail at relatively low mileage. Front suspension bushes: £200–£500. Most common across all Vaneo models.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	Engine Stalls at Low RPM (1.9 Petrol)
	Some 1.9 petrol Vaneos suffer from stalling at low RPM (60–120 rpm) in lower gears, particularly when pulling away. Often linked to the LPG system or throttle body. Diagnosis required; repair cost varies.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	6
	Door Seals and Gas Struts (Non-Engine)
	A common non-engine complaint — door seals fail, gas struts for the tailgate and sliding doors lose pressure, and the rear wash-wipe can leak into the headlining. Door seals: £100–£300; gas struts: £50–£150.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
________________


SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: Vaneo Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	Most Enquired Vaneo Engine
	OM668 1.7 CDI — cold-start stalling reports [PATTERN DATA]
	➡ Stable
	Second Most Enquired
	M166 1.6 petrol — ignition coil and misfire reports [PATTERN DATA]
	➡ Stable
	Typical UK Value
	£1,100–£1,900 [THIRD-PARTY]
	⬇ Falling
	Most Common Owner Profile
	Budget-conscious families and first-time buyers [PATTERN DATA]
	➡ Stable
	Insights from the data
* Interesting insight: The Vaneo is the cheapest used Mercedes you can buy — typical values of £1,100–£1,900 mean that a major engine repair (£1,200–£2,200) can exceed the car's value. Owners routinely scrap Vaneos with otherwise fixable engine problems because the repair cost doesn't make financial sense.
* The 1.7 CDI diesel is the most enquiry-generating engine — but the enquiries are almost exclusively about cold-start stalling and injector failure. The 1.6 petrol generates fewer enquiries because it's simpler, more reliable, and cheaper to fix. If you're buying a Vaneo, the petrol is the sensible choice; the diesel is the one to avoid unless you're prepared for the bills. 
* The Vaneo's front suspension is a more common cost driver than the engines — bushes, anti-roll bars, and suspension components fail at relatively low mileage. Many Vaneos are scrapped not because the engine failed, but because the suspension repair cost (£500–£1,000) exceeds the car's value. 
________________


SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every Vaneo Buyer
If you buy one Vaneo based on this page, buy a 2003–2005 facelift 1.6 petrol with full service history and suspension bushes already replaced. Avoid the 1.7 CDI diesel unless you're prepared for cold-start stalling and injector bills — the fuel economy isn't worth the headache. And never, ever spend more than the car is worth on an engine repair: a £1,500 Vaneo with a £1,800 repair bill is a write-off.

________________


The Vaneo rule — check the value first: A £800–£1,500 M166 recon against a £1,100–£1,900 Vaneo sits at 40–140% — the repair cost can exceed the car's value in many cases. The threshold for Mercedes-Benz is 70%: if the reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of what your car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. For a Vaneo worth £1,200 with a £1,200 OM668 recon, that's 100% — scrap it. For a Vaneo worth £1,900 with a £1,000 M166 recon, that's 52% — borderline, assess carefully. The Vaneo is the rare car where the decision to repair is almost entirely driven by the car's value, not the engine's condition.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE VANEO ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Vaneo Engine Range Evolved — 2001 to 2005
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Launch Era — W414 Introduction
	2001–2002
	M166 1.6 petrol (82 PS), OM668 1.7 CDI diesel
	The Vaneo was launched as a practical MPV based on the first-generation A-Class W168 platform — the engines were carried over from the A-Class with minimal modification
	The Vaneo was Mercedes' first serious attempt at a compact MPV — offering up to 3,000 litres of cargo space in a Golf-sized footprint
	Facelift Era — More Power
	2003–2005
	M166 1.6 petrol (102 PS), M166 1.9 petrol (125 PS), OM668 1.7 CDI diesel
	The 2003 facelift introduced more powerful petrol variants and revised interior trim — the 1.9 petrol was the most powerful Vaneo ever offered
	The 1.9 petrol (125 PS) was the pick of the range for motorway use — but it's rare; most Vaneos are the 1.6 petrol or 1.7 CDI. Production ended in 2005 with no direct successor.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A VANEO? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a Vaneo?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	★★★★☆
	The Vaneo is a practical, spacious MPV for families on a tight budget — 3,000 litres of cargo space, seven seats in some configurations, and a compact footprint. Buy a facelift 1.6 petrol (102 PS) with full service history and suspension work already done.
	Daily Commuter
	★★★☆☆
	The Vaneo is underpowered and not particularly economical for motorway commuting — the 1.7 CDI returns decent fuel economy but carries a cold-start stalling risk. The 1.6 petrol is cheaper to run in the city but drinks fuel on the motorway.
	Budget Owner
	★★★☆☆
	The Vaneo is the cheapest used Mercedes you can buy — £1,100–£1,900 for a decent example. But the purchase price is misleading; you'll need to budget for suspension repairs and routine maintenance. Buy the best example you can afford; a £1,900 car is cheaper in the long run than a £1,100 car with £1,000 of work needed.
	First-Time Buyer
	★★★☆☆
	The Vaneo is affordable and practical, but it's not a beginner-friendly car — suspension and engine issues can be expensive relative to the car's value. If you're a first-time buyer, consider the 1.6 petrol and get a pre-purchase inspection before committing.
	Practical MPV Buyer
	★★★★★
	If you need a spacious, practical MPV and your budget is £1,500–£2,000, the Vaneo is one of the best options available — more cargo space than a Zafira, more character than a Berlingo, and Mercedes build quality (mostly). Buy a facelift 1.6 petrol and enjoy the space.
	________________


SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My Vaneo Engine — or Should I Replace It?
The Vaneo's defining economics question is simple: is the repair cost worth more than the car? A £1,200 OM668 recon against a £1,200 Vaneo is 100% — scrap it. A £800 M166 recon against a £1,800 Vaneo is 44% — worth doing. Run the numbers on your specific car before deciding.

SECTION 13 — VANEO FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: Vaneo Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Vaneo engine replacement cost in the UK?
A used Vaneo engine costs £300–£1,200 depending on engine code; reconditioned units range from £800–£2,200 [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]. Labour adds 6–10 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). For a precise quote on your specific Vaneo, use our diagnostic calculator.
2. Which Vaneo engine is the most reliable?
The M166 1.6 petrol (82 PS or 102 PS) is the most reliable Vaneo engine — simple, well-understood, and cheaper to repair than the diesel. The 1.9 petrol (125 PS) is also reliable but rarer. The OM668 1.7 CDI diesel carries a cold-start stalling risk and injector issues on higher-mileage examples — it's the one to avoid unless you're prepared for the bills.
3. Which Vaneo engine should I avoid?
The OM668 1.7 CDI diesel is the one to avoid — cold-start stalling is the defining complaint, and injector failure can cost £300–£800 per injector. The 1.7 CDI is also more expensive to repair than the petrol variants, and many specialists won't touch them because the repair cost often exceeds the car's value.
4. Is the Vaneo timing chain problem real?
The Vaneo's M166 and OM668 engines use timing chains, not belts — chain failure is rare on the M166, but the OM668's chain tensioner can wear on higher-mileage examples. Listen for rattling on start-up; if you hear it, get it inspected. Chain replacement: £600–£1,200 if needed.
5. Is it worth replacing a Vaneo engine?
Apply the 70% Mercedes-Benz rule: if reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of what your car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. For a Vaneo worth £1,200 with a £1,200 OM668 recon, that's 100% — scrap it. For a Vaneo worth £1,900 with a £1,000 M166 recon, that's 52% — borderline, assess carefully. Use our Ownership Decision Tool for a personalised assessment.
6. Which Vaneo generation/year should I buy?
The definitive answer: a 2003–2005 facelift Vaneo 1.6 petrol (102 PS) with full service history and suspension work already done. The facelift improved interior trim and introduced the more powerful 1.6 petrol — and the suspension issues are the real cost driver, not the engines. Avoid the 1.7 CDI unless you're prepared for cold-start stalling and injector bills.
7. How do I find my Vaneo engine code?
The engine code is stamped on the engine block — typically on the left-hand side (driver's side in RHD cars) near the cylinder head. It's also on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in your service documentation. Common Vaneo codes: M166.961 (1.6, 82 PS), M166.960 (1.6, 102 PS), M166.990 (1.9, 125 PS), OM668.914 (1.7 CDI). If you're unsure, use our diagnostic calculator to identify it.
8. Vaneo vs A-Class — which is more reliable?
The Vaneo and A-Class W168 share the same platform and engines — reliability is essentially identical, but the Vaneo is heavier and more practical, which puts more strain on the suspension and running gear. The A-Class is cheaper to repair (lighter, simpler suspension) and has a larger parts network. If you need the space, buy the Vaneo; if you don't, buy the A-Class. See our Vaneo vs A-Class guide.
________________

Full engine replacement frequently approaches or exceeds the vehicle's commercial value — the economics of keeping a Vario on the road are fundamentally different from passenger car ownership.

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: Vario — One Generation, Two Powertrains
Sub-headline: The Vario was produced from 1996 to 2013 with a single body shell and two engine families.
________________


Vario (1996–2013)
BM667/668/670 — the full-size commercial van and medium-duty truck
1996–2013 • OM602, OM904
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The Vario is a genuine commercial vehicle — built for work, not weekend driving. The OM904 engine (introduced in 1996) is the one to buy: 4.25 litres, 122–177 hp, electronically controlled unit pump injectors, and proven in heavy-duty service. The earlier OM602 (2.87-litre five-cylinder) was carried over from the Sprinter but is less common in later Varios. Production ended in 2013 with no direct successor — parts availability is becoming a genuine concern for long-term ownership.

The Vario was available across a 4.8–7.5 tonne weight range with four wheelbase lengths from 3.15–4.80m. The same "Vario" badge covers vans, minibuses, chassis-cabs, dropside trucks, tippers, and box bodies. The engine is the same across variants, but the vehicle's value and replacement economics vary significantly by body type and condition.

Verification confirms the Vario used OM602 (five-cylinder) and OM904 (four-cylinder) — the OM366 is a 5.9-litre six-cylinder truck engine not fitted to the Vario.
Correction applied: The Vario's predecessor was the T2 (1986–1996), and the Vario was a facelifted version of that platform. The bodyshell remained relatively unchanged throughout production.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common Vario Engine Problems
Sub-headline: Six known failure patterns — drawn from specialist interviews, commercial vehicle forums, and documented Mercedes-Benz truck experience.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	Injector Failure (OM904)
	The electronically controlled unit pump injectors on the OM904 can fail, causing rough running, loss of power, and fuel dilution of the engine oil. Common on high-mileage examples. £300–£800 per injector replacement.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	2
	Fuel System Contamination
	The Vario's commercial use means fuel system contamination is a genuine risk — particularly on older examples with unknown service history. Clogged fuel filters and worn injectors are common causes of power loss. Regular filter changes are essential.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	3
	Glow Plug Failure (OM602/OM904)
	Hard starting or no-start in cold weather is often caused by faulty glow plugs. Common on high-mileage commercial vehicles that have been through many cold starts. £100–£300 glow plug replacement.
	🟢 Low
	Read more →
	4
	Oil Leaks
	Worn gaskets and seals can lead to oil leaks — a common issue on ageing Mercedes-Benz diesel engines. Low oil levels can lead to engine failure if ignored. Regular inspection is essential for commercial operators.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	5
	Turbocharger Issues (OM904)
	Turbocharger failure on the OM904 is typically caused by oil starvation or contaminated oil. Symptoms include loss of power, excessive smoke, and unusual noises. £800–£2,000 turbo replacement.
	🟠 Immediate
	Read more →
	6
	Parts Availability
	The Vario was discontinued in 2013 — parts availability is becoming a genuine concern for long-term ownership. Some components are shared with the Sprinter and LN2, but others are Vario-specific. Always check parts availability before committing to a major repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Read more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: Vario Market Intelligence — What UK Commercial Operators Told Us
Sub-headline: Drawn from documented commercial vehicle failure patterns, specialist interviews, and owner-forum consensus — the Vario is a genuine workhorse with a dedicated following.
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend

	➡ Stable — the OM904 remains the most common Vario engine in UK service
	OM602 parts availability
	[PATTERN DATA — declining]
	⬇ Falling — parts for the five-cylinder are becoming harder to source
	Vario body/chassis enquiries
	[PATTERN DATA — commercial]
	➡ Stable — Varios remain in service as minibuses, trucks, and specialist vehicles
	Commercial vehicle economics
	[PATTERN DATA — operator-dependent]
	➡ Stable — replacement decisions are driven by commercial value, not sentimental attachment
	Insights from the data:
Interesting insight: The Vario is a genuine commercial vehicle — not a passenger car, not a weekend toy. The economics of engine replacement are fundamentally different: a £2,500–£6,000 engine replacement against a £2,000–£8,000 vehicle value is a commercial decision, not an emotional one. For a minibus operator with 12 seats and 50,000 miles per year, a £4,000 engine replacement might be entirely justified. For a private owner with a backup vehicle, it probably isn't. 
The OM904 is the engine to buy — introduced in 1996 with electronically controlled unit pump injectors and oil change intervals of 45,000km, it proved itself in LN2 and Vario service. The OM602 was carried over from the Sprinter and is less common in later Varios. The OM904's Euro 3, 4 and 5 variants offer better emissions compliance and parts availability. 
The Vario was produced for 17 years with a single body shell — a remarkably long production run for any vehicle. This means later examples (2000–2013) share the same chassis and body as earlier ones, but benefit from improved engine electronics and emissions compliance. The 2000 model year introduced the 618D and 818D variants with direct injection — these are generally the ones to buy.

Check parts availability before committing to any major repair — the Vario was discontinued in 2013, and some components are becoming hard to find."

	£5,000–£8,000
	16–24 hrs
	Labour estimate: Fitting a Vario OM904 engine typically takes 16–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent commercial specialist) or £110–£160/hr (main dealer) [MB-QUOTE].
________________


The Vario rule — commercial vehicle economics are different:
A £2,500–£6,000 OM904 recon against a £2,000–£8,000 Vario sits at 31–300% of vehicle value — a far wider swing than any passenger car model. But unlike a passenger car, the Vario is a commercial vehicle: a £4,000 engine replacement in a £5,000 minibus with 12 seats and 50,000 miles per year might be entirely justified. The same £4,000 replacement in a £2,000 backup truck probably isn't.
The rule: Vario engine replacement decisions are commercial, not emotional. Calculate the vehicle's remaining commercial life, factor in parts availability (the Vario was discontinued in 2013), and consider whether a replacement vehicle offers better value. The OM904 is the engine to buy — but don't assume a £4,000 replacement is automatically justified just because the engine is available.
The Vario was discontinued in 2013 — parts availability is becoming a genuine concern. Before committing to any major repair, check the availability of major components (injectors, turbochargers, cylinder heads) for your specific engine variant. Some OM904 parts are shared with the LN2 and later Atego; others are Vario-specific and becoming harder to source.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE VARIO ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Vario Engine Range Evolved — 1996 to 2013
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Mercedes Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Vario Launch — OM602 Carryover
	1996–2000
	OM602 (2.87L 5cyl), OM904 (4.25L 4cyl)
	The Vario replaced the T2 (1986–1996) — the OM602 was carried over from the Sprinter; the OM904 was introduced as a more powerful option
	The OM904 was the engine Mercedes designed for the Vario and LN2 — the OM602 was the older design, carried over for lower-spec variants
	Euro 2 to Euro 3 Transition
	2000–2004
	OM904 (updated electronics)
	Emissions regulations drove the switch to electronically controlled unit pump injectors and improved engine management
	The OM904's unit pump injector system was advanced for its time — but it means injector failure is a £300–£800 repair per cylinder
	Euro 3 to Euro 5
	2004–2013
	OM904 (EGR, DPF on later variants)
	Euro 4 and Euro 5 emissions standards required EGR and, on some variants, DPF filters
	Late Varios (2006–2013) carry EGR and DPF systems — these add complexity and potential failure points
	End of Production
	2013
	OM904 (final variant)
	The Vario was discontinued in 2013 with no direct successor — the Sprinter had grown to cover much of the Vario's market
	The Vario's replacement was effectively the heavier Sprinter variants and the Atego light truck — but neither is a direct match for the Vario's 4.8–7.5 tonne weight range
	Key takeaway: The Vario's engine story is one of commercial vehicle evolution — from the carryover OM602 to the purpose-designed OM904, and from Euro 2 to Euro 5 emissions compliance. The OM904 is the engine to buy: introduced in 1996, proven in LN2 and Vario service, and available in Euro 3, 4 and 5 variants with increasing emissions complexity. The Vario was discontinued in 2013 — parts availability is the defining long-term concern for any owner.
________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A VARIO? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a Vario?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Commercial Operator / Minibus
	★★★★☆
	The Vario is a genuine commercial workhorse — the OM904 engine is robust and proven, and the 12–20 seat minibus variants are in high demand for school runs and community transport. Buy a well-maintained 2000+ example with OM904, check the service history, and budget for injector servicing at 100,000-mile intervals. The commercial value justifies a £3,000–£5,000 engine replacement if required.
	Commercial Operator / Truck
	★★★☆☆
	The Vario chassis-cab and dropside truck variants are proven workhorses — but the economics of engine replacement are tighter than minibus operators. A £2,500–£6,000 engine replacement against a £2,000–£6,000 truck value requires careful commercial assessment. Buy the OM904, check parts availability, and consider whether a replacement vehicle offers better value.
	Private Owner / Occasional Use
	★★☆☆☆
	The Vario is a lot of vehicle for private use — and a £2,500–£6,000 engine replacement against a £2,000–£4,000 vehicle value is a scrap decision for most private owners. Buy a Vario for private use only if you're comfortable with commercial vehicle economics and have a specialist who can maintain it.
	Collector / Restoration
	★★★☆☆
	The Vario has a dedicated following among commercial vehicle enthusiasts — the OM904's robustness and the Vario's 4.8–7.5 tonne weight capacity make it an interesting classic. But parts availability is becoming a concern. Buy a well-preserved example with a documented history, and budget for injector servicing and rust prevention.
	Specialist Conversion
	★★★★☆
	The Vario's 4.8–7.5 tonne capacity and available 3.15–4.80m wheelbase lengths make it a popular choice for motorhome, horsebox, and specialist vehicle conversions. The OM904 is the engine to buy — but check parts availability for your specific variant before committing to a conversion project.
	________________

SECTION 13 — VARIO FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: Vario Frequently Asked Questions
________________


Q1: How much does a Vario engine replacement cost in the UK?
A Vario engine replacement typically costs £2,000–£6,000 supply-only, depending on engine variant and emissions standard. The OM602 five-cylinder is the cheapest to replace (£2,000–£4,000 reconditioned [MB-QUOTE]); the OM904 four-cylinder Euro 4/5 is the most expensive (£3,000–£6,000 recon [MB-QUOTE]). Labour adds £910–£2,340 at independent rates. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised cost estimate.
________________


Q2: Which Vario engine is the most reliable?
The OM904 (introduced in 1996) is the most reliable Vario engine — 4.25 litres, 122–177 hp, electronically controlled unit pump injectors, and proven in LN2 and Vario service. The OM602 was carried over from the Sprinter and is less common in later Varios. Buy the OM904, check the service history, and budget for injector servicing at 100,000-mile intervals. Read the full OM904 guide →
________________


Q3: Which Vario engine should I avoid?
The OM602 is not a "avoid" engine — but it's the older design and parts are becoming harder to source. The OM602 was carried over from the Sprinter and is less common in later Varios. Buy a Vario with the OM904 if you can — it's the engine Mercedes designed for the Vario and LN2, with better parts availability and proven reliability.
________________


Q4: Is the Vario engine fuel system reliable?
The OM904's electronically controlled unit pump injector system is advanced for its time — but it means injector failure is a £300–£800 repair per cylinder. Regular fuel filter changes (every 30,000 miles) and the use of high-quality diesel fuel are essential for long-term reliability. Symptoms of injector failure: rough running, loss of power, and fuel dilution of the engine oil. If you notice any of these, book a specialist inspection immediately.
________________


Q5: Is it worth replacing a Vario engine?
The Vario was discontinued in 2013 — engine replacement decisions are commercial, not emotional.
A £2,000–£4,000 OM602 recon against a £2,000–£6,000 Vario sits at 33–200% of vehicle value — often a scrap decision unless the vehicle has significant commercial value.
A £2,500–£6,000 OM904 recon against a £2,000–£8,000 Vario sits at 31–300% — a far wider swing. For a minibus operator with 12 seats and 50,000 miles per year, a £4,000 engine replacement might be entirely justified. For a private owner with a backup vehicle, it probably isn't.
The rule: Calculate the vehicle's remaining commercial life, factor in parts availability, and consider whether a replacement vehicle offers better value. Use the calculator →
________________


Q6: Which Vario should I buy?
The 2000+ Vario with OM904 (Euro 3/4/5) is the one to buy. The OM904 is the most reliable engine, parts availability is better than the OM602, and later examples benefit from improved engine electronics and emissions compliance. The 2000 model year introduced the 618D and 818D variants with direct injection — these are generally the ones to buy. Check the service history, inspect the body for rust, and test the engine for injector noise and smooth running. See our full Vario Rankings →
________________


Q7: How do I find my Vario engine code?
Your Vario engine code can be found in three places:
1. VIN plate — located on the driver's side door pillar or under the bonnet. The engine code is usually in the 7th and 8th positions of the VIN.
2. Vehicle documentation — your V5C logbook lists the engine number; the engine code is the first part of that number.
3. Engine block stamping — on the OM904, the engine code is stamped on the left-hand side of the block near the front.
Don't rely on the model badge: A "614D" badge can mean OM602 or OM904 depending on year. Check the VIN or registration year to confirm. If you're unsure, enter your registration number into our calculator and we'll identify it for you. Use the calculator →
________________


Q8: Vario vs Sprinter — which is the better commercial vehicle?
The Vario (4.8–7.5 tonnes) and Sprinter (3.5–5.0 tonnes) serve different weight classes. The Vario is significantly heavier and carries larger payloads — but the Sprinter is more modern, better supported, and parts are easier to source.
The Vario offers 4.8–7.5 tonne capacity, 12–20 seat minibus options, and the proven OM904 engine — but production ended in 2013 and parts are becoming harder to source.
The Sprinter is available up to 5.0 tonnes, has a far larger parts network, and is still in production — but it cannot match the Vario's heavier payload capacity.
Our verdict: Buy the Vario if you need 4.8–7.5 tonnes capacity and can maintain a commercial vehicle no longer in production. Buy the Sprinter if you want modern reliability, better parts availability, and a vehicle that's still in production. Read the full comparison: Vario vs Sprinter →
________________
